proposals.title: Systematic Electronic Tuning of Bidentate Ligands for Iridium-Catalyzed C(sp3)-H Borylation: Correlating Hammett Parameters with Regioselectivity and Reactivity
proposals.motivation: Hartwig's group has pioneered the catalytic, regio- and stereoselective functionalization of C(sp3)-H bonds, particularly in amines and polymers. While ligand design is critical, systematic studies on the electronic effects of substituents on common bidentate ligand scaffolds (e.g., phenanthrolines, bipyridines) in these transformations are often limited to a few data points. A comprehensive, quantitative understanding of how electron-donating and withdrawing groups influence catalyst activity and selectivity would provide a powerful design principle for future catalyst development in C-H functionalization and polymer deconstruction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a linear free-energy relationship (LFER) exists between the electronic parameter (σ_p) of substituents on a bidentate ligand scaffold and the catalytic performance (rate, regioselectivity) in iridium-catalyzed C(sp3)-H borylation, enabling predictive catalyst optimization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of 4,7-disubstituted 1,10-phenanthroline ligands bearing a systematic range of para-substituted aryl groups with known Hammett constants. These ligands will be used in the iridium-catalyzed borylation of a model primary amine (e.g., n-octylamine) and a secondary C-H bond substrate under standardized conditions. Reaction rates (initial rates via in situ IR or quenching studies) and regioselectivity ratios (GC/MS, NMR) will be measured for each catalyst. Data will be analyzed by constructing Hammett plots (log(k_X/k_H) vs. σ_p) to quantify electronic effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, predictive framework for ligand electronic effects in Ir-catalyzed C-H borylation, moving beyond empirical observation. The resulting correlations will provide a rational blueprint for tailoring catalyst electronics to achieve desired reactivity and selectivity in small molecule and polymer functionalization, directly impacting the fields of C-H activation and chemical recycling.
proposals.novelty: While electronic effects are often discussed qualitatively, applying a rigorous Hammett analysis to a homogeneous, bidentate ligand library in Ir-catalyzed C(sp3)-H borylation of amines is unprecedented. This systematic approach contrasts with the more common practice of testing a small, ad hoc set of ligand variants.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed, Regioselective Intramolecular C(sp3)-H Amidation for the Synthesis of Saturated N-Heterocycles
proposals.motivation: The direct conversion of C-H bonds to C-N bonds via amidation is a powerful strategy for constructing nitrogen-containing heterocycles, which are ubiquitous in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. Prior to 2026, intermolecular C-H amination of C(sp3) centers has been achieved, but analogous intramolecular reactions for cyclization, particularly those controlling regioselectivity among multiple, similar C-H bonds, remain a significant challenge. Developing a catalytic system for such a transformation would provide a more efficient and step-economical route to valuable saturated N-heterocycles like pyrrolidines and piperidines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iridium catalyst, bearing a rationally designed bidentate ligand, can facilitate the intramolecular insertion of a nitrene equivalent into a specific, unactivated C(sp3)-H bond, enabling selective cyclization with control over both ring size and site-selectivity.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage principles from Hartwig's prior work on iridium-catalyzed, regio- and stereoselective C(sp3)-H silylation of amines. We will synthesize substrates featuring a pendant amide or sulfonamide group with a nitrene precursor (e.g., a tosyloxycarbamate). A library of iridium complexes with modified bidentate ligands (e.g., phenanthroline derivatives) will be screened to control the orientation and activation of the C-H bond. Key parameters include ligand sterics/electronics, the nature of the nitrene source, and the use of oxidants or directing groups to achieve high regioselectivity for 5- or 6-membered ring formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new catalytic method for the direct synthesis of saturated N-heterocycles from simple linear precursors, expanding the toolbox of C-H functionalization for heterocycle synthesis. It will provide fundamental insights into the factors governing regioselectivity in intramolecular nitrene C-H insertion, a critical step toward achieving catalyst-controlled site-selectivity in complex molecules.
proposals.novelty: While intermolecular C-H amination is known, applying the selectivity principles of iridium-catalyzed C-H silylation to the distinct challenge of intramolecular C-H amidation for cyclization is unexplored. The novelty lies in achieving predictable, catalyst-controlled regioselectivity for ring closure, moving beyond substrate-controlled bias.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed, Site-Selective C(sp3)-H Amidation of Complex Drug-Like Molecules via Silyl Nitrene Transfer
proposals.motivation: Late-stage functionalization (LSF) of complex molecules is a powerful strategy for diversifying drug candidates and optimizing their properties. Pre-2026, direct C-H amidation methods for installing nitrogen into unactivated aliphatic chains remain limited, especially for substrates with many competing C-H bonds. A general, catalytic method for the selective amidation of C(sp3)-H bonds in medicinally relevant scaffolds would enable rapid synthesis of novel analogs from advanced intermediates.
proposals.hypothesis: An iridium catalyst system, previously developed for the silylation of C(sp3)-H bonds in amines, can be adapted to catalyze the intermolecular amidation of diverse, complex molecules using a silyl nitrene precursor. We hypothesize that ligand-controlled sterics and electronics will override innate bond strengths to achieve high site-selectivity on drug-like substrates.
proposals.method: We will adapt the reported Ir-catalyzed silylation conditions (e.g., [Ir(cod)OMe]2 with bipyridine or phenanthroline ligands) to use a silyl azide (e.g., TMSN3) as a nitrene precursor. The reaction will be tested on a panel of complex, nitrogen-containing pharmaceuticals (e.g., derivatives of lidocaine, propranolol, or steroids) containing primary, secondary, and tertiary C-H bonds. High-throughput experimentation (HTE) will screen ligand libraries and additives to optimize yield and regioselectivity. The site of amidation will be determined by 2D NMR and X-ray crystallography.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new catalytic protocol for the direct installation of amide groups into aliphatic C-H bonds of highly functionalized molecules. It will provide a valuable tool for medicinal chemists to rapidly generate nitrogen-rich analogs. The study will also deliver fundamental insights into the factors governing site-selectivity in late-stage C-H functionalization of congested molecular architectures.
proposals.novelty: While Hartwig's group has demonstrated directed C-H silylation of simple amines, applying this catalytic manifold to intermolecular, undirected amidation of complex, polyfunctional substrates is unexplored. The key novelty lies in achieving predictable, catalyst-controlled selectivity in molecules with numerous, sterically and electronically similar C-H bonds, moving beyond simple model systems.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed Intramolecular C–H Amination via C–C Bond Cleavage for the Synthesis of N-Heterocycles
proposals.motivation: Intermolecular C–H amination via metal nitrenoid insertion is a powerful method for C–N bond formation, but controlling regioselectivity in complex molecules remains challenging. An intramolecular variant, particularly one initiated by C–C bond cleavage, would offer a novel disconnection to access strained and functionalized N-heterocycles, motifs prevalent in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. This approach merges the strategic power of C–C bond activation with the efficiency of C–H functionalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iridium catalyst, paired with a suitable oxidant and ligand, can generate a reactive nitrenoid intermediate from a tethered amine-containing substrate, triggering a sequential C–C bond cleavage and intramolecular C–H amination to form nitrogen heterocycles. The tether will enforce proximity and control regioselectivity.
proposals.method: We will design substrates featuring a primary amine tethered via a carbon chain (e.g., -CH2-CH2-) to a cyclobutanol or analogous strained alcohol. Using pre-2026 Hartwig group expertise, we will employ [Ir(COD)OMe]2 with electron-deficient phenanthroline ligands under oxidative conditions (e.g., PhI(OAc)2) to convert the amine to a nitrenoid. The key step involves β-carbon elimination from the in-situ generated metal alkoxide of the strained alcohol, cleaving the C–C bond and generating an iridium-bound enolate, which then undergoes intramolecular C–H amination. Reaction parameters (tether length, ligand, oxidant) will be optimized via high-throughput experimentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new catalytic manifold for ring-expansive N-heterocycle synthesis, directly converting simple amines and strained rings into valuable lactams or cyclic amines. It will demonstrate the feasibility of merging C–C bond cleavage with C–H amination in a single catalytic cycle, expanding the synthetic toolkit for complex molecule construction. The methodology will provide a complementary route to traditional cyclization methods.
proposals.novelty: While intermolecular C–H amination and catalytic C–C bond cleavage of strained rings are known, their merger into an intramolecular, cascade process initiated by nitrenoid formation is unprecedented. The novelty lies in using the energy of nitrene formation to drive the C–C cleavage step, creating a new disconnection for N-heterocycles.

proposals.title: Solubility-Controlled, Iridium-Catalyzed C–C Bond Functionalization: Replacing Alkyl Chains with C–O, C–S, and C–P Bonds via Heteroatom Nucleophiles
proposals.motivation: The Hartwig group's recent development of a solubility-control strategy for the catalytic silylation and borylation of C(sp3)–H bonds and polymer backbones (e.g., polyethylene, PVC) enables the functionalization of traditionally inert materials. However, this powerful approach has been limited to forming C–Si and C–B bonds. Extending this methodology to directly install C–O, C–S, and C–P bonds would provide a transformative, catalytic route to deconstruct and valorize polyolefins and other saturated hydrocarbons into oxygenates, thioethers, and phosphines, which are valuable as chemical feedstocks and functional materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The solubility-control principle—where a substrate is rendered insoluble to facilitate selective surface-like reactivity—can be generalized beyond amination/silylation to enable the catalytic replacement of C–C bonds in polymers and small molecules with C–heteroatom bonds using O-, S-, and P-centered nucleophiles under mild conditions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established iridium catalyst systems, employing tailored bidentate ligands (e.g., phenanthroline derivatives) to modulate oxidative addition into C–C bonds and subsequent functionalization. Nucleophiles such as alcohols/thiols for C–O/C–S formation and secondary phosphines for C–P formation will be evaluated. The key innovation is applying the solubility-control strategy: for polymer substrates like polyethylene, reactions will be conducted in solvents where the polymer is insoluble, confining catalysis to the polymer-solvent interface to control site-selectivity and prevent over-functionalization. Small-molecule model systems (e.g., long-chain alkanes) will be used to optimize conditions before scaling to polymeric materials.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general platform for catalytic C–C bond heterofunctionalization, moving beyond C–H activation. It will provide direct synthetic methods to convert inert hydrocarbon feedstocks into valuable heteroatom-functionalized molecules and materials. Success would represent a major advance in polymer deconstruction and sustainable synthesis, enabling new pathways from waste plastics to adhesives, surfactants, or ligands.
proposals.novelty: While catalytic C–C bond cleavage for borylation or amination is emerging, its extension to a broad suite of oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus nucleophiles via a unified solubility-control mechanism is unexplored. Applying this strategy to install these heteroatoms directly into polyolefin backbones constitutes a novel approach to polymer editing.

proposals.title: Catalytic Installation of Amine and Boronate Ester Functional Groups via C–H Functionalization for Tunable Polyolefin Deconstruction and Upgrading
proposals.motivation: Recent work from the Hartwig group has demonstrated that installing oxime or ester pendent groups via C–H functionalization enables the catalytic deconstruction of polyolefins via Beckmann rearrangement or hydrogenolysis. However, the scope of accessible polymer properties and degradation pathways is limited by this narrow set of functional handles. Expanding the toolkit of pendent groups is critical to tailor material properties (e.g., adhesion, compatibility) and to unlock new, selective chemical recycling strategies for plastic waste.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that primary amines and alkyl pinacol boronates can be installed as pendent groups on model polyolefins and commercial polyethylene via iridium-catalyzed, site-selective C–H borylation followed by functional group interconversion, and that these new functional groups will enable distinct polymer properties and deconstruction pathways compared to esters and oximes.
proposals.method: The approach will employ established Ir-catalyzed C–H borylation conditions (e.g., [(cod)Ir(OMe)]2 with bipyridine or phenanthroline ligands) on polyolefin substrates to install boronate esters. These intermediates will be converted to primary amines via a copper-catalyzed Chan-Lam-Evans-type amination with hydroxylamine derivatives. Alternatively, the boronate esters will be retained as pendent groups. The modified polymers will be characterized by NMR, GPC, and thermal analysis. Their deconstruction will be explored via established protocols: acidolysis or oxidation for boronates, and Hofmann elimination or other N-elimination reactions for the amine-functionalized polymers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish catalytic methods to diversify the functional group inventory on inert polyolefins, directly expanding the scope of Hartwig's 'backbone editing' paradigm. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships linking pendent group identity to material behavior and will demonstrate new, selective depolymerization pathways, contributing to the broader goal of achieving chemical circularity for plastics.
proposals.novelty: While C–H borylation of polyolefins is known, its use to install nitrogen-based nucleophiles (amines) and the systematic study of boronate esters as permanent, reactive pendent groups for property modulation represent unexplored avenues for polymer editing, moving beyond the established ester/oxime strategy for chain cleavage.

proposals.title: Catalytic, Site-Selective Interconversion of Ketone and Alcohol Functionalities in Polyketone and Polyol Backbones via Ligand-Controlled Transfer Hydrogenation and Dehydrogenation
proposals.motivation: The ability to edit polymer backbones post-polymerization is a powerful strategy for tuning material properties, but methods for the direct, selective interconversion of functional groups within a polymer chain are rare. Inspired by recent work on polymer deconstruction via catalytic C–N bond formation (e.g., Beckmann rearrangement), we propose to develop catalytic methods for the selective reduction of ketones to alcohols or oxidation of alcohols to ketones in polymer chains. This would enable the precise modification of commodity polymers like polyketones or poly(vinyl alcohol) derivatives without requiring full depolymerization, offering a new paradigm in polymer functionalization and recycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that well-defined transition metal catalysts, guided by tailored ancillary ligands, can achieve chemoselective and potentially site-selective transfer hydrogenation or dehydrogenation reactions on ketone or alcohol functionalities embedded within a polymer backbone, without causing chain scission or undesired side reactions.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Hartwig group's expertise in C–H borylation and iridium catalysis to develop new systems. Initial focus will be on iridium complexes bearing tunable, electron-donating N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) or phosphine ligands for transfer hydrogenation of model polyketone oligomers using isopropanol as the hydrogen donor. Conversely, for alcohol oxidation, we will explore ruthenium or iridium pincer complexes for acceptorless dehydrogenation or using ketone acceptors. Reactions will be monitored by NMR, GPC, and mass spectrometry to assess selectivity, conversion, and polymer integrity. Successful conditions will be applied to higher molecular weight polymers, including modified polyethylene derivatives accessible via prior borylation/oxidation sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general catalytic methods for the direct interconversion of carbonyl and alcohol groups in polymer main chains. It will contribute fundamental principles of catalyst design for polymer substrate selectivity and demonstrate a new, efficient approach to diversifying polymer properties from a single precursor. This expands the toolkit of 'polymer editing' beyond C–H functionalization and C–C bond cleavage.
proposals.novelty: While catalytic hydrogenation/dehydrogenation of small molecules is mature, its application for selective functional group interconversion within an inert polymer backbone is unexplored. The novelty lies in adapting these reactions to operate on macromolecular substrates with high chemoselectivity, preventing side reactions like over-reduction or β-hydride elimination that would degrade the chain.

proposals.title: Direct, Site-Selective C–H Functionalization of Polyethylene via Hydrogen Atom Transfer and Metallaphotoredox Catalysis
proposals.motivation: The upcycling of polyethylene (PE) waste into higher-value materials requires methods to install functional handles directly onto its inert, aliphatic backbone. Pre-2026 approaches, such as the Hartwig group's work on catalytic borylation of PVC or PE deconstruction via Beckmann rearrangement, often require pre-oxidation or chain cleavage. A direct C–H functionalization strategy that bypasses oxidation and preserves the polymer chain would represent a more atom-economical and versatile platform for PE valorization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic combination of a hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) catalyst and a nickel/photoredox dual catalytic system can directly functionalize secondary C(sp3)–H bonds in model and commercial polyethylene with nitrogen- or carbon-based groups under mild, visible-light conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ decatungstate as a HAT photocatalyst to abstract hydrogen from PE, generating a carbon-centered radical. This radical will be intercepted by a nickel catalyst under dual photoredox catalysis, using an iridium photocatalyst (e.g., [Ir(dF(CF3)ppy)2(dtbbpy)]PF6) to drive the nickel cycle. The nickel complex, ligated by tailored bidentate nitrogen ligands, will couple the polymer radical with electrophilic partners such as aryl halides (for C–C bond formation) or O-benzoyl hydroxylamines (for C–N bond formation). Reactions will be performed on low-molecular-weight PE models and commercial HDPE in solvent mixtures at room temperature with blue LED irradiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general catalytic method for direct C–H hetero-functionalization of polyethylene, creating a versatile toolbox for installing polar functional groups. It will demonstrate how metallaphotoredox catalysis, a powerful method in small-molecule synthesis, can be translated to the selective modification of commodity polymers, opening new pathways to adhesive, compatibilized, or degradable materials from waste PE.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox C–H functionalization is known for small molecules, its application to unactivated, solid polyolefins is unexplored. This approach is distinct from prior Hartwig group methods as it targets direct C–H to C–X conversion on PE without requiring backbone oxidation, deconstruction, or the installation of a pre-existing heteroatom handle.

proposals.title: Ligand Field Engineering of Base Metal Complexes for Aqueous C–N Coupling: A Bioinspired Strategy to Replace Palladium Catalysts
proposals.motivation: Palladium-catalyzed C–N bond formation is foundational in organic synthesis but suffers from high cost, toxicity, and sensitivity to aqueous conditions, limiting its sustainability and application scope. The development of base metal catalysts for such transformations remains a major challenge, as nickel and copper complexes often lack the necessary stability and selectivity under aqueous, aerobic conditions. This project addresses the critical need for sustainable, cost-effective alternatives to precious metal catalysis in amination reactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing ligand architectures inspired by the precise first- and second-coordination sphere environments of metalloenzymes, we can create stable, well-defined nickel or copper complexes capable of catalyzing C–N bond formation in water, thereby directly replacing palladium catalysts in aqueous amination reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of polydentate, redox-active ligands featuring heterocyclic donors (e.g., pyridines, imidazoles) and secondary-sphere hydrogen-bonding motifs, drawing inspiration from enzymes like copper amine oxidases and nickel-containing urease. These ligands will be complexed with Ni(II) and Cu(II) salts. The resulting complexes will be screened in model aqueous amination reactions (e.g., coupling of aryl halides with amines). Advanced spectroscopic techniques (EPR, XAS) and DFT calculations will be used to characterize electronic structure, oxidation state changes, and ligand field effects, guiding iterative ligand optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for base metal catalyst design by translating principles of bioinorganic ligand field control to synthetic molecular catalysis. It will provide a generalizable strategy for replacing Pd in challenging aqueous-phase bond-forming reactions, contributing directly to the fields of sustainable catalysis and C–H/N–H functionalization. A successful catalyst would demonstrate that sophisticated ligand design can overcome the inherent limitations of base metals for demanding catalytic transformations.
proposals.novelty: While ligand design for base metals is known, the systematic application of bioinspired, enzyme-like ligand field engineering to achieve aqueous-phase C–N coupling—a domain dominated by Pd—is a novel and non-obvious approach. This moves beyond simple ligand substitution to create a tailored electronic microenvironment that mimics nature's control over base metal reactivity.

proposals.title: Base-Metal-Catalyzed Aqueous C–N Coupling: Replacing Palladium with Nickel Complexes for Sustainable Amine Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Palladium-catalyzed C–N coupling is a cornerstone of modern organic synthesis but suffers from high cost and toxicity, limiting industrial scalability. The development of aqueous-phase reactions is a key sustainability goal, yet base-metal catalysts for such transformations remain underdeveloped. Replacing Pd with abundant Ni or Cu in this challenging, protic medium would address both economic and environmental constraints in pharmaceutical and agrochemical manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that carefully designed, electron-rich, nitrogen-based ligands can stabilize Ni(II)/Ni(0) or Cu(I)/Cu(III) cycles in water, enabling them to catalyze the oxidative amination of aryl halides with primary and secondary amines, a reaction dominated by Pd. The aqueous environment may uniquely favor base-metal catalysis by modulating redox potentials and suppressing deleterious side reactions.
proposals.method: We will screen a library of bidentate and tridentate nitrogen ligands (e.g., phenanthrolines, pyridylimines, and N-heterocyclic carbenes with solubilizing groups) in combination with Ni(OTf)2 and CuI salts. Reactions will be performed in water or water/cosolvent mixtures under air or controlled atmospheres, using aryl bromides and iodides with diverse amines. High-throughput experimentation (HTE) will identify lead systems, followed by detailed mechanistic studies (kinetics, EPR, XAS) to elucidate the catalytic cycle and the role of water. Ligand design will focus on achieving both electron donation and aqueous solubility.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general, aqueous-phase C–N coupling protocol catalyzed by nickel or copper, providing a cost-effective and sustainable alternative to palladium. It will expand the toolbox of base-metal catalysis in challenging media and offer fundamental insights into stabilizing reactive intermediates in water. The resulting methodology could directly impact green chemical synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While Ni/Cu catalysis in organic solvents is known, their application to *aqueous* C–N coupling is virtually unexplored and non-trivial due to catalyst decomposition and hydrolysis challenges. The proposal uniquely combines Hartwig's expertise in C–N bond formation and ligand design with the emerging theme of polymer deconstruction via C–N bonds, suggesting potential for circular chemistry applications.

proposals.title: Ligand-Free, Site-Selective C–N Coupling via Palladium Atoms Confined in Functionalized Mesoporous Silica Nanoreactors
proposals.motivation: Palladium-catalyzed C–N coupling is a cornerstone of synthetic chemistry, but it universally requires expensive, air-sensitive phosphine ligands to control selectivity and prevent catalyst deactivation. This reliance increases cost, complexity, and environmental footprint. A ligand-free system that achieves similar selectivity through physical confinement and engineered surface interactions would represent a transformative advance in sustainable catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that atomically dispersed palladium sites immobilized within the tailored nanopores of a functionalized hydrophilic support will act as ligand-free nanoreactors, where the confined environment and specific surface functional groups (e.g., wall-bound ammonium or amine moieties) will control the palladium coordination sphere and reaction pathway, enabling selective Buchwald-Hartwig amination.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of mesoporous silica (SBA-15) supports with tailored surface functionalities (e.g., -NH3+, -NH2, -SO3-). Palladium will be introduced via strong electrostatic adsorption or grafting to achieve atomic dispersion, confirmed by HAADF-STEM and XAS. These materials will be evaluated as catalysts for the amination of aryl halides with primary and secondary amines in aqueous/organic solvent mixtures. Reaction scope, selectivity, and kinetics will be studied and compared to homogeneous ligand-controlled systems. Catalyst stability and leaching will be rigorously assessed via hot filtration and ICP-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for ligand-free cross-coupling by demonstrating that spatial confinement and surface engineering can replace traditional ligand roles in controlling selectivity. It will provide fundamental insights into the coordination geometry and mechanism of palladium in constrained environments, potentially leading to more robust and easily separable catalysts for practical synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While ligand design for homogeneous Pd catalysis is mature, the concept of completely eliminating the ligand and replacing its function with a designed nanoconfined solid interface for C–N coupling is unprecedented. This directly challenges a core assumption of the field by drawing an analogy from environmental heterogeneous catalysis, where immobilized metals perform selective transformations without soluble ligands.

proposals.title: A Cooperative Bimetallic System for the Nickel-Catalyzed Suzuki-Miyaura Cross-Coupling of Aryl Fluorides
proposals.motivation: Aryl fluorides are exceptionally abundant and stable building blocks, yet their use in cross-coupling is severely limited by the high strength of the C–F bond. Current methods for activating aryl C–F bonds typically require forcing conditions, specialized catalysts, or activated substrates, which limits functional group tolerance and synthetic utility. Developing a general, mild catalytic method to functionalize aryl fluorides would unlock a vast, underutilized chemical feedstock for synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cooperative bimetallic system, pairing a Lewis acidic metal to pre-activate the C–F bond with a nickel catalyst for oxidative addition, will enable the mild and selective cross-coupling of unactivated aryl fluorides under conditions comparable to those used for aryl chlorides.
proposals.method: We will develop a Ni/M bimetallic system, initially focusing on Ni(0)/Al(III) or Ni(0)/B(III) pairs. The Lewis acid (e.g., Al alkyls or B-based electrophiles) will be screened for its ability to coordinate the fluoride, weakening the C–F bond. Concurrently, we will optimize Ni(0) precatalysts bearing electron-rich, sterically demanding N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) or phosphine ligands to facilitate oxidative addition. Key additives, such as alkoxide bases and fluoride scavengers (e.g., Me3SiOTf), will be employed to manage fluoride release and prevent catalyst deactivation. The system will be tested in Suzuki-Miyaura couplings of 4-fluorobenzonitrile and related substrates with aryl boronic acids.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for C–F bond activation via cooperative catalysis, moving beyond single-metal strategies. It will provide a practical, mild method to directly utilize simple aryl fluorides in cross-coupling, significantly expanding the synthetic toolbox. The fundamental insights into managing fluoride ions in catalytic cycles will be broadly applicable to other transformations involving strong σ-bonds.
proposals.novelty: While bimetallic strategies are known for C–H activation, their application to the selective activation of the stronger C–F bond in cross-coupling is underexplored. The novelty lies in the engineered synergy between a fluoride-abstracting Lewis acid and a tuned Ni catalyst to achieve reactivity under conditions previously reserved for much more labile aryl halides.

proposals.title: Nickel-Catalyzed C–F Amination of Aryl Fluorides in Aqueous Media Enabled by Cooperative Ligand Design
proposals.motivation: Aryl fluorides are abundant, chemically inert feedstocks, yet their direct conversion to anilines via C–F amination remains a significant challenge, especially under sustainable conditions. Pre-2026, state-of-the-art methods for C–F functionalization rely heavily on precious metals (e.g., Pd, Rh) and anhydrous organic solvents. Developing a catalytic system using earth-abundant metals in water would address key economic and environmental shortcomings in this important bond-forming transformation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a nickel catalyst, supported by a tailored electron-donating and water-compatible ligand, can facilitate oxidative addition into the strong C–F bond and subsequent C–N coupling in aqueous media, where water may act as a beneficial proton shuttle or ligand.
proposals.method: We will screen a library of bidentate phosphine and N-heterocyclic carbene ligands, inspired by Hartwig's prior work on C–H borylation and silylation, for their ability to stabilize Ni(0)/Ni(II) cycles in water. Catalytic reactions will employ aryl fluorides and primary/secondary amines in water/co-solvent mixtures. Key parameters include pH control, temperature, and the potential use of synergistic metal additives (e.g., Zn) to facilitate transmetalation. Reaction progress will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR, with mechanistic studies (kinetic isotope effects, stoichiometric oxidative addition experiments) probing the role of water.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general method for base metal-catalyzed C–F amination in water, directly contributing to green chemistry and the valorization of fluorinated arenes. It will provide fundamental insights into how protic environments influence the oxidative addition and reductive elimination steps at nickel centers, guiding future catalyst design for challenging bond activations.
proposals.novelty: While nickel-catalyzed C–N couplings of aryl halides are known, the combination of targeting the strongest aryl–halogen bond (C–F) and performing the catalysis in aqueous media with a base metal is unprecedented, merging three major challenges—bond strength, metal scarcity, and solvent sustainability—into one catalytic system.

proposals.title: Ligand-Controlled Metal Hydroxide Complexes as Selective Inorganic Bases for C–H Functionalization
proposals.motivation: In aqueous-phase catalysis, hydroxide is the default inorganic base, but its high reactivity and lack of structural definition often limit selectivity in complex substrates. Metalloenzymes, such as zinc hydrolases, achieve precise selectivity by embedding a metal-bound hydroxide within a tailored protein environment. Translating this bioinspired principle to synthetic catalysis could unlock new selectivity paradigms for challenging bond-forming reactions in water.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic metal-ligand complexes, where a hydroxide or alkoxide is held within a defined, tunable coordination sphere, will act as superior selective bases compared to simple hydroxide, enabling regio- and stereoselective functionalization of C–H bonds in amines and other substrates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of well-defined iridium and palladium complexes bearing ancillary ligands (e.g., pincer-type, bidentate nitrogen donors) with a coordinated hydroxide or alkoxide. These complexes will be evaluated as catalysts or co-catalysts in model C–H borylation and amination reactions of primary and secondary amines in aqueous or protic media. Reaction outcomes (regioselectivity, stereoselectivity, yield) will be correlated with structural features of the complexes (pKa, steric parameters, ligand donor strength) to establish design principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of tunable, structurally defined inorganic bases for catalysis, moving beyond simple salts. It will provide fundamental insights into how a metal-coordinated base's microenvironment controls selectivity, bridging bioinorganic concepts with synthetic methodology. The resulting catalysts could enable more efficient and selective transformations of amine-containing polymers and small molecules.
proposals.novelty: While ligand design is central to transition-metal catalysis, its application to create a *tunable inorganic base* for controlling selectivity in aqueous C–H functionalization is unexplored. This approach directly challenges the assumption that the base in such systems is merely a stoichiometric reagent.

proposals.title: Palladium-Catalyzed Amination of Aryl Iodides with Aqueous Ammonia Enabled by KPhos Ligands
proposals.motivation: The direct, catalytic conversion of aryl halides to primary anilines using aqueous ammonia is a highly desirable but challenging transformation, as it provides a streamlined route to valuable nitrogen-containing building blocks. Prior to 2026, successful systems for aryl bromides and chlorides using bulky biarylphosphine ligands like KPhos have been developed, but aryl iodides often present unique reactivity profiles and side reactions, limiting the general scope. Establishing a robust protocol for aryl iodides would significantly expand the synthetic utility of this atom-economical amination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the steric and electronic properties of the KPhos ligand family, which have proven effective for less reactive aryl chlorides, can be tuned to suppress β-hydride elimination and other decomposition pathways common with aryl iodides, enabling their efficient amination with aqueous ammonia.
proposals.method: We will systematically screen Pd precursors (e.g., Pd(OAc)2, Pd2(dba)3) with a library of KPhos ligands (KPhos, KPhos2, KPhos3) in the amination of model aryl iodides using concentrated aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen source. Reaction parameters such as temperature, base (e.g., K3PO4, NaOt-Bu), solvent (toluene, dioxane), and ammonia concentration will be optimized. The scope will then be evaluated with electronically diverse and functionalized aryl iodides, with reaction progress monitored by GC-MS and NMR spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a general, practical method for synthesizing primary anilines from readily available aryl iodides, complementing existing methods for bromides and chlorides. It will deepen the understanding of ligand-controlled reactivity in Pd-catalyzed amination, specifically regarding the stability of arylpalladium amido intermediates derived from the most reactive halide substrates.
proposals.novelty: While KPhos ligands are known for amination of aryl chlorides/bromides, their specific application to tame the high but unselective reactivity of aryl iodides with aqueous ammonia is unexplored and non-obvious, as iodides typically require different catalyst design to prevent off-cycle decomposition.

proposals.title: Systematic Ligand Design for Micellar Cross-Coupling: Aqueous Suzuki-Miyaura Reactions of Polyhalogenated and Polycyclic Aryl Halides
proposals.motivation: The development of sustainable cross-coupling reactions in water remains a major goal, but the extremely poor solubility of many industrially relevant, highly hydrophobic substrates (e.g., polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, heavily functionalized aryl halides) in aqueous media presents a fundamental barrier. While micellar catalysis, using surfactants like TPGS-750-M, can solubilize such substrates, current approaches often rely on standard ligands optimized for organic solvents, leading to suboptimal performance. There is a critical need to understand and optimize ligand structure for catalysis at the unique micellar interface to achieve practical, high-yielding aqueous cross-couplings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic variation of KPhos-type ligand architecture—specifically tuning steric bulk, electron density, and local hydrophobicity—will reveal profound and predictable effects on catalytic activity and selectivity in micellar Suzuki-Miyaura reactions, as these parameters govern catalyst partitioning, substrate access, and the stability of key intermediates at the micelle-water interface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of KPhos ligands with systematic variations in the biaryl backbone, phosphine substituents, and pendant group hydrophobicity. These ligands will be paired with palladium precursors and screened in the aqueous micellar Suzuki-Miyaura coupling of challenging, highly hydrophobic substrates (e.g., 2-chloronaphthalene, polyhalogenated biphenyls) using TPGS-750-M as the surfactant. High-throughput experimentation (HTE) will be employed to rapidly assess reaction yields and selectivities, correlating performance with quantified ligand descriptors (e.g., %VBur, Hammett parameters, logP) to establish structure-activity relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design principles for ligands operating specifically in micellar catalytic environments, moving beyond empirical discovery. It will deliver new, high-performance catalyst systems for coupling recalcitrant hydrophobic substrates in water, advancing the practical application of sustainable cross-coupling. The resulting structure-activity relationships will provide a blueprint for designing next-generation catalysts for other aqueous organometallic transformations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in using micelles for solubility, but in the deliberate, systematic redesign of privileged ligand scaffolds to optimize their function *within* the constrained, amphiphilic environment of a micelle, a parameter space largely unexplored in ligand design prior to 2026.

proposals.title: Mediator-Controlled Electrochemical Regeneration of Palladium Catalysts for Selective C–H and C–N Oxidative Coupling
proposals.motivation: Electrochemical methods for Pd-catalyzed oxidative cross-couplings offer a sustainable alternative to chemical oxidants. However, direct anodic oxidation of Pd(0) often requires high potentials that degrade sensitive functional groups and complex substrates, limiting synthetic utility. A general strategy to achieve mild, selective electrochemical catalyst regeneration would significantly broaden the scope of these powerful transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing a tailored redox mediator to shuttle electrons between the anode and the Pd catalyst will decouple the catalyst oxidation potential from the electrode interface. This will enable the selective regeneration of Pd(II) under mild conditions, suppressing deleterious side reactions and expanding functional group tolerance in oxidative Pd catalysis.
proposals.method: We will design and screen a library of organic (e.g., TEMPO derivatives, phenoxazines) and inorganic (e.g., [Fe(Cp)2]+/0) redox mediators, evaluating their oxidation potentials and stability. These mediators will be integrated into electrochemical C–H arylation and C–N coupling reactions using well-defined Pd complexes with nitrogen-based ligands. Key metrics include mediator turnover number, Faradaic efficiency, product yield, and functional group survival compared to direct anodic oxidation. Advanced electrochemical analysis (cyclic voltammetry, bulk electrolysis) will map the mediator-Pd redox cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general platform for mediator-controlled electrochemical Pd catalysis, enabling the use of lower catalyst loadings and more complex substrates. It will provide fundamental insights into electron transfer processes in catalytic networks and deliver practical, selective methods for constructing C–C and C–N bonds under mild electrochemical conditions.
proposals.novelty: While redox mediators are known in organic electrosynthesis, their systematic application to control the oxidation state and selectivity of a transition metal catalyst like Pd in cross-coupling is unexplored. This approach moves beyond simple anodic regeneration to create a tunable, two-stage electron transfer system.

proposals.title: Photoredox-Catalyzed Deaminative Halogenation of Primary Arylamines via N-Centered Radicals and Halogen Atom Transfer
proposals.motivation: Primary arylamines are ubiquitous building blocks, but their direct conversion to aryl halides, essential synthetic intermediates, remains a significant challenge. Classical methods like the Sandmeyer reaction require diazotization and generate stoichiometric waste. A catalytic, oxidative C-N bond cleavage strategy would provide a more efficient and sustainable route to aryl halides from readily available aniline feedstocks, aligning with modern goals of atom economy and step efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual photoredox/halogen atom transfer (XAT) catalytic system can overcome the thermodynamic barrier of C-N bond cleavage in primary arylamines by coupling endergonic N-centered radical formation to exergonic aryl radical halogenation, enabling a net oxidative deaminative halogenation.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual catalytic system featuring an iridium-based photocatalyst (e.g., [Ir(dF(CF3)ppy)2(dtbbpy)]PF6) to oxidize the arylamine substrate via single-electron transfer (SET) upon blue LED irradiation. This generates an aminyl radical cation, poised for N-centered radical formation and C-N bond homolysis. Concurrently, the reduced photocatalyst will activate a halogen atom transfer (XAT) catalyst, such as a silyl or germane radical mediator (e.g., (TMS)3Si–H with N-iodosuccinimide), to deliver a halogen atom (Br, I) to the resulting aryl radical, yielding the aryl halide product and closing both catalytic cycles. Ligand effects on the iridium photocatalyst and the structure of the XAT mediator will be systematically optimized.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, catalytic paradigm for converting primary arylamines to aryl halides, bypassing diazonium intermediates. It will expand the synthetic utility of photoredox catalysis by demonstrating its power to drive challenging bond-cleavage reactions through radical relay mechanisms. Furthermore, it will provide fundamental insights into the reactivity of N-centered radicals generated via oxidative SET.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox catalysis has been used for C-H functionalization and halogenation, its application to direct, catalytic C-N bond cleavage in simple primary amines via N-centered radicals is unprecedented. The explicit coupling of photoredox-generated oxidants to a XAT cycle for deaminative functionalization represents a novel disconnection strategy.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed Retro-Amination: Electrochemically Driven C-N Bond Cleavage of Primary Arylamines to Aryl Halides
proposals.motivation: The development of catalytic methods for C-N bond cleavage is a significant challenge, complementary to the well-established C-N coupling reactions pioneered by the Hartwig group. Such methods would enable the deconstruction of amine-containing polymers and the valorization of nitrogen-rich waste streams, as highlighted by recent work on polyolefin deconstruction. A general, catalytic strategy to convert robust aryl amines back to aryl halides, valuable synthetic intermediates, would create a reversible, sustainable chemical cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: That the thermodynamic and kinetic parameters of an iridium-catalyzed C-H amination cycle can be systematically inverted by controlling redox potentials and halogen source, enabling the selective, catalytic cleavage of primary aryl C-N bonds to form aryl halides.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of electrochemical analysis and catalyst engineering. First, cyclic voltammetry will be used to map the redox potentials of key Ir intermediates in known amination cycles. Ligand libraries, focusing on electron-deficient phosphines and N-heterocyclic carbenes, will be screened to stabilize high oxidation states favorable for retro-reduction. The reaction will be driven using an electrochemical cell or chemical oxidants (e.g., N-fluoropyridinium salts) paired with halogen sources (e.g., LiCl, NaBr). Sacrificial olefin acceptors will be tested to trap the released nitrogen moiety as an aziridine, pulling the equilibrium.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general, catalytic method for the direct conversion of primary arylamines to aryl halides, a formal retro-amination. It will provide fundamental insight into the reversibility of organometallic elementary steps involved in C-N bond formation. The strategy could transform the end-of-life processing of nitrogen-containing materials and enable new synthetic disconnections.
proposals.novelty: While enzymatic retro-hydroxylation is known in biocatalysis, applying this 'reverse catalysis' principle to a transition-metal-catalyzed C-H functionalization manifold for C-N cleavage is unprecedented. The approach moves beyond stoichiometric deaminative methods by aiming for a fully catalytic, redox-driven cycle.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed Deaminative Halogenation of Primary Arylamines via C-N Bond Cleavage
proposals.motivation: Primary arylamines are ubiquitous motifs in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals, but methods for their direct, catalytic conversion to aryl halides—key synthetic handles for cross-coupling—are underdeveloped. Current deamination strategies often rely on diazonium salts, which pose safety and stability concerns. A catalytic, mild, and regioselective deaminative halogenation would provide a valuable retrosynthetic disconnection for arene functionalization.
proposals.hypothesis: The iridium catalyst system, previously developed by the Hartwig group for the regioselective silylation of C(sp3)-H bonds in primary amines, can be operated in reverse under modified conditions to catalytically cleave the robust C(aryl)-N bond of primary arylamines, enabling their direct conversion to aryl halides.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established iridium catalyst (e.g., [Ir(OMe)(cod)]2) and phenanthroline-based ligand framework used for C-H silylation. The reaction will be tested with arylammonium salts or in situ-generated amine derivatives (e.g., N-alkylpyridinium salts) as activated substrates. Key variables include the halide source (e.g., alkali metal halides, TMS-X), oxidant (for catalyst turnover if needed), and solvent. High-throughput experimentation will screen ligand and additive combinations to favor C-N cleavage over side reactions.
proposals.contribution: This work would establish the first catalytic method for the direct deaminative halogenation of primary arylamines, expanding the synthetic utility of amine functionalities. It would demonstrate the reversibility of a powerful C-H functionalization manifold, providing a new strategic approach to polymer deconstruction and the editing of complex molecules.
proposals.novelty: While C-N bond formation from aryl halides is routine, the catalytic deconstruction of a primary arylamine to an aryl halide is a fundamentally underexplored inverse transformation. Applying a C-H functionalization catalyst system in reverse for selective bond cleavage is a non-obvious and conceptually novel strategy.

proposals.title: Substrate-Controlled, Chemoselective N-Arylation via a Removable Bidentate Directing Group: A General Route to Asymmetric Diarylamines
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of asymmetric diarylamines, key motifs in pharmaceuticals and materials, remains challenging due to the poor chemoselectivity in classical Ullmann-type couplings. The high nucleophilicity of ammonia and primary anilines relative to secondary anilines leads to over-arylation and symmetric byproducts, necessitating stoichiometric directing groups or harsh conditions. A general catalytic method to enforce selective mono-arylation of primary anilines over ammonia and self-coupling is a significant unsolved problem in N-arylation chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: Installing a removable bidentate directing group (e.g., an 8-aminoquinoline or picolinamide derivative) onto the aryl electrophile will enforce chemoselectivity by coordinating a metal catalyst (Cu, Ni, or Pd) to create a tailored pocket that selectively positions a primary aniline for C–N coupling while sterically and electronically disfavoring ammonia coordination and bis-arylation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize aryl electrophiles (e.g., aryl halides, pseudohalides) bearing a removable bidentate directing group (DG). These will be screened in cross-coupling with primary anilines using Hartwig group expertise in ligand design for Cu, Ni, and Pd catalysts. Key parameters include ligand structure (e.g., bulky phosphines, N-heterocyclic carbenes), base, and solvent to optimize the DG-metal complex for selective primary aniline coupling. Post-coupling, the directing group will be cleaved under mild, orthogonal conditions (e.g., hydrolysis, photolysis) to yield the asymmetric diarylamine. Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, substrate-controlled strategy for synthesizing asymmetric diarylamines with high chemoselectivity and yield, minimizing symmetric byproduct formation. It will expand the toolbox for C–N bond formation by introducing a directing group paradigm typically used in C–H functionalization into the context of cross-coupling chemoselectivity challenges.
proposals.novelty: While directing groups are common in C–H functionalization, their application to enforce chemoselectivity in classical cross-coupling by modifying the electrophile, rather than the nucleophile, is underexplored. This approach inverts the traditional strategy and leverages substrate control to solve a fundamental selectivity problem in N-arylation.

proposals.title: Ligand-Gated, Regioselective C–N Coupling: A Catalytic System for the Selective Arylation of Aniline over Ammonia
proposals.motivation: The selective formation of diarylamines via C–N coupling is a critical transformation in pharmaceutical and materials synthesis. However, achieving high selectivity for coupling aniline over simpler, more nucleophilic amines like ammonia remains a significant challenge, as most catalysts favor the more reactive small nucleophile. Developing a catalyst-controlled system that overrides inherent nucleophilicity to achieve substrate-specific coupling would represent a major advance in synthetic methodology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transition metal catalyst, paired with a sterically demanding and electronically tunable ancillary ligand, can create a binding pocket that selectively stabilizes the transition state for aniline coupling through shape complementarity and secondary interactions, while sterically excluding ammonia, thereby inverting the typical selectivity based on nucleophilicity.
proposals.method: We will employ a ligand design strategy inspired by Hartwig's prior work on sterically demanding ligands for C–H borylation and silylation. A modular bisphosphine or P,N-ligand scaffold with tunable aryl substituents and hydrogen-bond acceptor/donor groups will be synthesized. These ligands will be screened in iridium- or palladium-catalyzed coupling reactions between aryl halides and mixtures of aniline and ammonia. High-throughput experimentation will be used to map the relationship between ligand structure (steric parameters, electronic properties, and H-bonding motifs) and the aniline/ammonia selectivity ratio.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of 'ligand-gated' catalysts that provide unprecedented selectivity in amine coupling reactions. It will establish design principles for achieving substrate specificity in small-molecule catalysis, moving beyond traditional reactivity paradigms. The resulting methodology will enable more efficient syntheses of complex diarylamine-containing molecules.
proposals.novelty: While ligand design for steric and electronic control is well-established, applying it to create an enzyme-like binding pocket for discriminating between two small, nucleophilic amines based on size and secondary interactions in a standard coupling reaction is a novel and unexplored strategy. This approach directly transplants a core principle of enzymatic selectivity into mainstream transition metal catalysis.

proposals.title: Ligand-Enabled Iridium Catalysis for the Low-Temperature C(sp3)-H Borylation of Polyolefins and Amines
proposals.motivation: The catalytic functionalization of inert C(sp3)-H bonds in abundant feedstocks like polyolefins and simple amines is a major challenge, often requiring harsh conditions (>300°C) that limit functional group tolerance and energy efficiency. Hartwig's recent work on polymer deconstruction and C-H borylation demonstrates the potential for creating value-added materials from waste, but milder, more selective catalysts are needed for practical applications. Achieving high conversion at sub-300°C temperatures would enable the precise chemical recycling of plastics and the late-stage diversification of amine-containing pharmaceuticals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tailoring the steric and electronic properties of bidentate phosphine or phenanthroline-based ligands on iridium centers will lower the kinetic barrier for C-H oxidative addition, enabling efficient catalytic turnover at temperatures between 150-250°C. This ligand design will be particularly effective for the C-H bonds in polyethylene and primary alkyl amines.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of electron-rich, sterically hindered bidentate ligands, building on pre-2026 successes with ligands like dtbpy and BINAP. These will be complexed with [Ir(OMe)(COD)]2. Catalytic screening will be performed using high-throughput experimentation (HTE) to test C-H borylation of model substrates (e.g., octane, hexylamine) and real polyethylene/poly(vinyl chloride) samples. Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored by GC-MS, NMR, and GPC. The most promising catalysts will be studied mechanistically via stoichiometric reactions, NMR spectroscopy, and DFT calculations to correlate ligand parameters with catalytic activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of Ir catalysts capable of operating under unprecedentedly mild conditions for the C-H borylation of saturated hydrocarbons and amines. It will provide fundamental structure-activity relationships linking ligand design to catalytic performance in challenging C-H functionalization. The project will directly advance the fields of catalytic polymer upcycling and synthetic methodology for amine functionalization.
proposals.novelty: While Ir-catalyzed C-H borylation is known, achieving high conversion on recalcitrant substrates like polyethylene at temperatures below 250°C is unprecedented. The novelty lies in the systematic ligand-driven approach to overcome the high kinetic barriers of these specific, unactivated C(sp3)-H bonds, merging concepts from polymer deconstruction and small-molecule catalysis.

proposals.title: Operando Spectroscopic Elucidation of the Catalyst-Polymer Interface in Iridium-Catalyzed C–H Borylation of Polyolefins
proposals.motivation: The catalytic functionalization of inert C–H bonds in polymers, as demonstrated in recent borylation and silylation work, offers a powerful route to upcycle plastic waste into value-added materials. However, the mechanisms at the solid-liquid interface between heterogeneous polymer substrates and homogeneous catalysts remain poorly understood, limiting rational optimization. Employing operando spectroscopy to directly observe this interface and key intermediates would provide unprecedented mechanistic insight into these transformative reactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that operando spectroscopic monitoring will reveal that the rate-determining step and regioselectivity in iridium-catalyzed polyolefin borylation are governed by specific, reversible interactions between the catalyst and the polymer chain at the interface, which can be modulated by ligand design.
proposals.method: We will employ a suite of complementary operando techniques. Reaction progress will be monitored using in-situ attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy to track the disappearance of polymer C–H bonds and formation of C–B bonds in real time. Simultaneously, in-situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) at the Ir L3-edge will be used to probe changes in the oxidation state and coordination geometry of the iridium catalyst. These experiments will be conducted in a specially designed reaction cell that maintains catalytic conditions while allowing spectroscopic interrogation of the polymer-catalyst slurry.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first direct, molecular-level view of the active species and polymer binding events in catalytic polymer C–H functionalization. It will establish structure-activity relationships linking ligand architecture to interfacial interactions and catalytic efficiency, guiding the design of next-generation catalysts for plastic deconstruction and functionalization.
proposals.novelty: While operando methods are common in heterogeneous catalysis, their application to homogeneous catalysts acting on solid polymer substrates is rare. This approach uniquely targets the dynamic, poorly understood solid-liquid interface, moving beyond inferences from model small-molecule studies to observe the actual polymer-catalyst system under working conditions.

proposals.title: Catalyst-Controlled Override of BDE-Directed Selectivity for the Sterically Favored C–H Amidation of Aliphatic Amines
proposals.motivation: Catalytic C–H functionalization of amines is a powerful strategy for late-stage diversification, but selectivity is typically governed by bond dissociation energy (BDE), favoring functionalization at weaker, less hindered C–H bonds (e.g., 3° > 2° > 1°). This intrinsic bias limits access to valuable, sterically congested motifs. Developing catalysts that can invert this selectivity to target stronger but more accessible methylene or methyl sites would enable new synthetic disconnections and streamline the synthesis of complex amine derivatives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sterically demanding, electron-tunable iridium catalyst, paired with a bulky nitrene precursor, can override BDE-driven selectivity by imposing a steric control regime, directing amidation to less hindered primary and secondary C–H bonds even in the presence of weaker tertiary C–H bonds.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of sterically encumbered, electron-deficient pyridine-oxazoline (PyOx) and phosphine-oxazoline ligands to modulate the steric and electronic properties of the iridium catalyst. These catalysts will be evaluated in the amidation of model substrates containing competing primary, secondary, and tertiary C–H bonds using bulky sulfonyl azides (e.g., 2,4,6-triisopropylbenzenesulfonyl azide) as nitrene sources. High-throughput experimentation and kinetic analysis will identify catalyst-substrate pairs where the selectivity ratio (k_primary/k_tertiary) inverts from <1 to >1, indicating successful steric override.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general principle and practical toolkit for overriding BDE-based selectivity in C–H functionalization, moving from innate reactivity to catalyst-controlled site-selectivity. It will provide direct access to previously disfavored, sterically accessible amine derivatives, expanding the utility of C–H amidation in synthetic planning. The developed ligand families will offer a new design strategy for selectivity inversion in other metal-catalyzed C–H transformations.
proposals.novelty: While steric effects are known to influence selectivity, deliberately designing a catalyst system to systematically invert the fundamental BDE hierarchy in C–H amidation of aliphatic amines is unprecedented. This approach merges Hartwig group expertise in sterically controlled C–H borylation/silylation with the challenge of overriding the strong thermodynamic driver inherent to nitrene insertion.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed, Regioselective Silylation of C(sp3)-H Bonds in Long-Chain and Branched Alkanes
proposals.motivation: The direct, catalytic functionalization of unactivated C(sp3)-H bonds in simple alkanes remains a formidable challenge in synthetic chemistry, with most successful systems limited to small, gaseous substrates (e.g., methane, ethane) or substrates with directing groups. The recent Hartwig group report on iridium-catalyzed silylation of C-H bonds in primary amines demonstrates a powerful system for selective C-H bond cleavage. Extending this catalytic manifold to larger, liquid alkanes would provide a transformative tool for converting abundant hydrocarbon feedstocks into value-added organosilicon building blocks under mild conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The iridium catalyst system, employing a sterically demanding, electron-donating ligand (e.g., a phenanthroline derivative) and a silane reagent, can be adapted to cleave and functionalize the strong, unactivated C-H bonds in larger alkanes (C5-C12) with regioselectivity dictated by the catalyst, overcoming the inherent reactivity bias of the substrate.
proposals.method: We will employ the optimized iridium precursor (e.g., [Ir(OMe)(cod)]2) and bidentate nitrogen ligands from the amine silylation work. The reaction will be tested with linear (pentane, octane, dodecane) and branched (isooctane, methylcyclohexane) alkanes using tertiary silanes like Et3SiH. High-throughput experimentation (HTE) with parallel pressure tubes will screen ligand structures, temperatures (100-150 °C), and alkane/silane ratios to maximize conversion and regioselectivity. Products will be characterized by GC-MS, NMR, and deuterium-labeling studies to determine site-selectivity and kinetic isotope effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general, catalytic method for the direct silylation of unfunctionalized, long-chain alkanes, moving beyond model substrates. It will provide fundamental insights into the steric and electronic parameters governing catalyst-controlled regioselectivity in alkane functionalization. Success would unlock a new disconnection for synthesizing alkylsilanes from petroleum derivatives.
proposals.novelty: While iridium systems are known for borylation and silylation of directed substrates, their application to achieve catalyst-controlled regioselectivity in undirected, complex alkanes is unprecedented. The novelty lies in translating a system developed for amine-directed C-H cleavage to the vastly more challenging context of unbiased alkane C-H bonds.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed Deconstructive Hydrolysis of N-Alkyl Amides via C–N Bond Cleavage
proposals.motivation: The catalytic formation of C–N bonds, particularly in amides, is a cornerstone of synthetic chemistry. However, the selective catalytic cleavage of robust alkyl C–N bonds in amides remains a significant challenge, limiting strategies for chemical recycling and the deconstruction of nitrogen-containing polymers and pharmaceuticals. Developing a catalytic, mild method for this transformation would enable new approaches to chemical circularity and feedstock recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iridium catalyst system, analogous to those used for C–H silylation and borylation, can be adapted to catalyze the reductive cleavage of the C–N bond in N-alkyl amides, converting them back to the constituent alkane and amide precursors under hydrogenative conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ iridium complexes bearing electron-donating, sterically hindered N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC) or phosphine ligands, which have proven effective for C–H functionalization in Hartwig group work. The catalytic system will be tested under hydrogen pressure (1-10 atm) with potential hydride sources or silanes as stoichiometric reductants. Reaction optimization will focus on solvent, temperature, and ligand effects, using model substrates like N-octyl acetamide. Success will be evaluated by NMR and GC-MS analysis for alkane and carboxylic acid amide formation.
proposals.contribution: This work would establish the first general, catalytic method for the reductive cleavage of alkyl C–N bonds in amides, a formal inverse of amide alkylation. It would provide a foundational tool for the deconstruction of polyamides and other N-containing materials, contributing to sustainable chemistry by enabling closed-loop chemical cycles from waste back to valuable feedstocks.
proposals.novelty: While catalytic C–N bond formation is well-established, catalytic C(alkyl)–N cleavage in amides under mild, reductive conditions is virtually unexplored. Leveraging C–H activation catalyst systems for this inverse deconstructive process is a novel and non-obvious strategy.

proposals.title: Copper-Free, Photoredox-Catalyzed C–N Bond Formation via Radical Activation of Aryl Halides
proposals.motivation: Copper is ubiquitously employed as a co-catalyst in palladium-catalyzed C–N cross-couplings, such as the Buchwald-Hartwig amination, to facilitate the critical oxidative addition of aryl halides. This requirement adds cost, complicates ligand design, and introduces a toxic heavy metal. Developing copper-free protocols using alternative activation modes would streamline catalyst systems and expand the sustainability profile of these foundational transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aryl halides can undergo productive oxidative addition to palladium(0) via single-electron transfer pathways initiated by a photoredox catalyst, generating aryl radical intermediates and circumventing the need for a copper(I) additive.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual catalytic system combining a strongly reducing iridium(III) photoredox catalyst (e.g., [Ir(dF(CF3)ppy)2(dtbbpy)]PF6) with a palladium(0) complex bearing electron-rich, sterically hindered biarylphosphine ligands (e.g., BrettPhos, RuPhos). Under blue LED irradiation, the excited photoredox catalyst will reduce aryl bromides or iodides, generating aryl radicals and a Pd(I) species. Subsequent radical capture and recombination will form the key Pd(II)–Ar intermediate, enabling the standard cross-coupling cycle with amines. Reaction parameters (light intensity, ligand, solvent, base) will be optimized using high-throughput experimentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, copper-free platform for C–N bond formation, directly challenging a long-standing paradigm in cross-coupling. It will merge photoredox catalysis with transition metal catalysis in a novel mechanistic pathway, potentially enabling couplings with substrates sensitive to copper. The findings will contribute fundamental insights into single-electron oxidative addition processes.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox/Pd dual catalysis is known for C–C bond formation, its application to copper-free C–N coupling via radical aryl halide activation is unexplored. The proposal uniquely targets the replacement of a stoichiometric metal additive with a photochemical step, a significant departure from established catalytic amination manifolds.

proposals.title: Cobalt-Catalyzed, Regioselective C(sp3)-H Silylation of Primary Amines via Nitrene Intermediates
proposals.motivation: The Hartwig group's recent iridium-catalyzed C(sp3)-H silylation of amines is a powerful method for late-stage functionalization, but reliance on a scarce, precious metal limits its scalability and industrial potential. Replacing Ir with an earth-abundant first-row transition metal is a critical challenge in sustainable catalysis, aligning with the field's push towards cost-effective and environmentally benign transformations. Success would significantly enhance the practical utility of this valuable C–H bond functionalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a well-defined cobalt(II) complex, supported by a redox-active or strongly donating pincer ligand, can catalyze the intramolecular silylation of primary amine C–H bonds via a nitrene intermediate generated from a silyl azide precursor. The ligand's ability to modulate the cobalt oxidation state and stabilize high-energy intermediates will be key to matching Ir's performance.
proposals.method: The approach will focus on synthesizing a library of cobalt complexes bearing bis(imino)pyridine, pyridine diimine, and phosphine-based pincer ligands. These catalysts will be screened in the silylation of model primary amines using (trimethylsilyl)azide. Reaction optimization will involve systematic variation of solvent, temperature, and silane/azide equivalents. High-throughput experimentation and in-situ spectroscopic monitoring (IR, NMR) will be employed to identify active species and optimize turnover. Mechanistic studies, including radical clock probes, kinetic isotope effects, and isolation of proposed cobalt-nitrene species, will validate the operative pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general, earth-abundant metal-catalyzed system for direct, intramolecular C(sp3)-H silylation of amines, a reaction currently exclusive to precious metals. It will expand the toolbox of sustainable C–H functionalization and provide fundamental insights into the reactivity of base-metal nitrenoids. The developed catalytic system could be broadly applicable to other nitrene-mediated C–H amination and functionalization reactions.
proposals.novelty: While cobalt-catalyzed nitrene transfer for C–H amination is known, its application to C–H silylation—a reductive process following C–N bond formation—with amines as directing groups is unprecedented. The key novelty lies in leveraging cobalt's propensity for one-electron redox steps to achieve the challenging reductive elimination of a C–Si bond from a putative high-valent metal-nitrene/silyl intermediate.

proposals.title: Systematic Development of Pyridyl-Imidazoline Ligand Scaffolds for Enhanced Regioselectivity in Iridium-Catalyzed C(sp3)-H Borylation
proposals.motivation: The Hartwig group's recent development of iridium-catalyzed, regio- and stereoselective silylation of C(sp3)-H bonds in amines represents a significant advance in functionalizing unreactive aliphatic C-H bonds. However, achieving high selectivity across diverse amine substrates remains a challenge, and ligand structure is a primary determinant of catalytic performance. A focused study to optimize the pyridyl-imidazoline ligand scaffold, which has shown promise in these systems, is crucial for expanding the synthetic utility and substrate scope of this powerful transformation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic modification of the pyridyl-imidazoline ligand's steric and electronic properties—through variation of substituents on the imidazoline ring and the pyridyl backbone—will provide a rational ligand library that significantly improves reaction rates and regioselectivity in the catalytic borylation of primary and secondary aliphatic C-H bonds.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of pyridyl-imidazoline ligand analogs by varying key structural elements: (1) steric bulk at the imidazoline C2 position (H, Me, iPr, tBu, aryl), (2) electronic character via substitution on the pyridyl ring (electron-donating/-withdrawing groups at the 4- and 5-positions), and (3) backbone rigidity via incorporation of fused ring systems. These ligands will be screened in the iridium-catalyzed borylation of model primary and secondary amine substrates (e.g., N-Boc-pentylamine, N-Boc-piperidine) using pre-2026 established conditions. Catalytic performance (conversion, rate, regioselectivity) will be evaluated via GC-MS and NMR spectroscopy, with the most promising ligands tested on more complex substrates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear structure-activity relationships for the pyridyl-imidazoline ligand class in C(sp3)-H borylation, providing a roadmap for ligand selection for challenging transformations. It will deliver new, superior ligands that enable more efficient and selective functionalization of amine-containing molecules, directly impacting synthetic methodology for pharmaceutical and polymer science. The findings will also inform ligand design principles for other challenging bond-forming reactions catalyzed by late transition metals.
proposals.novelty: While the parent pyridyl-imidazoline ligand is known, a systematic investigation into the effect of its precise steric and electronic modulation on the demanding selectivity of primary vs. secondary C(sp3)-H bond activation in amines has not been reported. This proposal moves beyond single ligand demonstration to a principled exploration of the ligand scaffold's potential.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Role of Ligand Architecture in Steric Control of C–H Borylation Regioselectivity in Five-Membered Heterocycles
proposals.motivation: The Hartwig group's iridium-catalyzed C–H borylation is a powerful tool for functionalizing inert bonds, but achieving predictable, high regioselectivity in unactivated substrates like five-membered rings remains a challenge. While empirical ligand design has led to successes, the precise mechanistic origin of steric control—how ligand structure dictates site-selectivity through non-covalent interactions—is poorly understood, limiting the rational design of catalysts for complex molecules.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high regioselectivity in five-membered rings is governed not by simple steric bulk, but by specific, directional non-covalent interactions (e.g., C–H···π, dispersion forces) between the ligand scaffold and the substrate transition state, which can be systematically mapped and quantified.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined experimental and computational approach. A series of iridium catalysts bearing systematically varied, yet structurally related, bidentate bipyridine and phenanthroline ligands will be synthesized and evaluated in the borylation of model saturated five-membered heterocycles (e.g., tetrahydrofuran, pyrrolidine). Detailed kinetic studies, including variable-temperature and isotopic labeling experiments, will be coupled with high-level DFT calculations (using M06-2X or ωB97X-D functionals with dispersion correction) to model the C–H activation transition states. Non-covalent interaction (NCI) analysis and distortion/interaction–activation strain models will be used to deconvolute the energetic contributions of steric repulsion and attractive dispersion forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed, mechanistic blueprint for how ligand architecture enforces regioselectivity in C–H functionalization of challenging small rings. It will move the field from empirical correlation to predictive models based on quantifiable non-covalent interactions, directly informing the design of next-generation catalysts for the selective editing of complex molecular backbones, relevant to polymer deconstruction and functionalization.
proposals.novelty: While ligand effects are often attributed to sterics, this study uniquely focuses on dissecting and quantifying the attractive dispersion interactions within the steric envelope that are critical for selectivity in constrained, five-membered ring systems, a nuance not deeply explored in prior borylation mechanistic studies.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed, Regioselective C(sp3)-H Silylation of Complex Pharmaceuticals and Natural Products Containing Five-Membered Heteroarenes
proposals.motivation: Late-stage functionalization (LSF) of complex molecules is a powerful strategy for diversifying pharmaceuticals and optimizing their properties, but direct, selective C-H functionalization on drug-like scaffolds remains challenging.
