Pre-2026, Hartwig's group has developed an iridium-catalyzed system for the regio- and stereoselective silylation of C(sp3)-H bonds in simple amines, a valuable transformation as silyl groups serve as versatile synthetic handles. Applying this methodology directly to complex, functionalized molecules would address a significant bottleneck in medicinal chemistry and chemical biology.
proposals.hypothesis: The catalyst system, employing an iridium precursor and a tailored bisphosphine ligand, will catalyze the selective silylation of primary and secondary C-H bonds proximal to five-membered heteroarenes (e.g., pyrroles, imidazoles, thiophenes) in complex molecular architectures, tolerating a wide array of sensitive functional groups present in pharmaceuticals.
proposals.method: We will screen the reported iridium catalyst (e.g., [Ir(OMe)(cod)]2) and bisphosphine ligands (e.g., dtbpy) for silylation (using HSiMe(OR)2) on a panel of complex substrates derived from commercial drugs and natural products containing five-membered heteroarenes. Reaction conditions (temperature, solvent, silane) will be optimized for each substrate class using high-throughput experimentation techniques. The regioselectivity and functional group tolerance will be rigorously assessed via NMR and LC-MS, and the synthetic utility of the installed silyl groups will be demonstrated through derivatization (e.g., Tamao-Fleming oxidation to alcohols).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, catalytic method for the direct, late-stage silylation of C-H bonds in medicinally relevant heteroarene-containing scaffolds, significantly expanding the toolbox for lead diversification. It will provide fundamental insights into the steric and electronic constraints of this transformation on highly functionalized molecules and deliver new, selectively modified analogues of bioactive compounds for property evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While C-H silylation of simple substrates is known, its application to the direct, catalyst-controlled functionalization of complex pharmaceuticals containing sensitive heterocycles is unprecedented and non-obvious, moving beyond model systems to solve a practical synthetic problem.

proposals.title: Enantioselective C–H Borylation of Aliphatic Amines via Supramolecular Metal-Organic Cage Catalysts
proposals.motivation: Achieving high levels of enantioselectivity in the functionalization of unactivated C(sp3)–H bonds, particularly in flexible substrates like aliphatic amines, remains a formidable challenge in synthetic chemistry. Traditional small-molecule catalysts often lack the precise three-dimensional steric environment needed to discriminate between prochiral faces or methylene groups. This project seeks to address this by designing enzyme-inspired catalytic pockets using supramolecular chemistry, a powerful but underexplored approach for C–H activation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chiral, self-assembled metal-organic cage, constructed from iridium centers and tailored bridging ligands, will create a confined microenvironment that can bind primary amine substrates via secondary interactions and enforce enantioselective borylation at a specific methylene C–H bond.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of chiral, ditopic pyridine- or phenanthroline-based ligands with amide or urea functionalities for substrate recognition. These will be combined with iridium precursors known for C–H borylation (e.g., [Ir(COD)(OMe)]2) to self-assemble into M4L6 or M6L8 tetrahedral cages. Catalytic screening will focus on the desymmetrization of meso-diamines or prochiral primary amines. Selectivity will be tuned by systematically varying the ligand's backbone sterics, hydrogen-bonding motifs, and chiral elements, guided by computational modeling (DFT) of substrate binding modes within the cage.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for enantioselective C–H functionalization by translating principles of supramolecular coordination chemistry into catalysis. It will provide fundamental insights into how confined spaces can control reactivity and selectivity in bond-forming reactions relevant to the synthesis of chiral amines. Success would expand the toolbox for polymer deconstruction and valorization, aligning with Hartwig's recent work on catalytic polymer editing.
proposals.novelty: While iridium-catalyzed C–H borylation is well-known, applying self-assembled chiral cages to enforce enantioselectivity in amine C–H functionalization is unprecedented. The novelty lies in merging the substrate-directing ability of amines with a supramolecular catalyst's steric confinement, moving beyond traditional ligand design to create a catalytic 'active site'.

proposals.title: Continuous Flow Process for the Iridium-Catalyzed, Regioselective C(sp3)-H Silylation of Primary Amines: Enabling Safe and Scalable Synthesis of Organosilanes
proposals.motivation: The direct, catalytic silylation of unactivated C-H bonds in amines, as pioneered by Hartwig's group, provides a powerful route to valuable organosilanes. However, the reported batch reactions require elevated temperatures and pressures of hydrogen gas, posing significant safety and scalability challenges for industrial application. Transitioning this transformation to a continuous flow platform would mitigate these risks by enabling precise control over hazardous conditions and improving heat and mass transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the iridium-catalyzed, regioselective silylation of primary and secondary C(sp3)-H bonds in primary amines can be efficiently conducted in a continuous flow reactor, leading to improved safety, scalability, and potentially enhanced reaction rates and selectivity compared to batch conditions.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a high-pressure, high-temperature continuous flow reactor system equipped with back-pressure regulators. Initial studies will focus on optimizing key flow parameters—including residence time, temperature, pressure, catalyst concentration, and ligand-to-metal ratio—for model substrates using the established (POCOP)Ir catalyst system. The system will be engineered to safely handle gaseous H2 and volatile silane reagents. Reaction progress and purity will be monitored in-line via IR spectroscopy and off-line by GC-MS and NMR.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first continuous flow protocol for the direct C-H silylation of amines, a significant advance in operational safety for this powerful bond-forming reaction. It will establish a scalable and practical method for synthesizing amine-derived organosilanes, important building blocks in materials science and pharmaceuticals. Furthermore, the study will provide general insights into adapting high-pressure, gas-liquid-solid catalytic C-H functionalizations for continuous manufacturing.
proposals.novelty: While continuous flow C-H borylation has been explored, the translation of the analogous, more challenging silylation reaction—which involves distinct mechanistic steps and safety concerns with H2 gas and pyrophoric silanes—to a continuous platform is unprecedented. The integration of this specific, high-value iridium-catalyzed transformation with flow chemistry represents a novel and non-obvious engineering solution.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed, Controlled Depolymerization of Polyamides and Polyurethanes via Bifunctional Ligands with Intramolecular Proton Shuttles in Aprotic Media
proposals.motivation: Chemical recycling of condensation polymers like nylons and polyurethanes is crucial for a circular plastics economy, but current hydrolytic or solvolytic methods often require protic solvents, limiting thermal stability and compatibility with advanced catalysts. Aprotic solvents offer wider thermal windows and compatibility with moisture-sensitive catalysts but fail to provide the proton stabilization needed for controlled cleavage of amide or urethane bonds, leading to poor selectivity and catalyst poisoning. A general catalytic strategy to enable controlled depolymerization in purely aprotic media remains a significant unmet challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing iridium catalysts with bifunctional ligands containing a tunable, pendant proton donor (e.g., a phenol or ammonium group) will provide intramolecular, transition-state stabilization for the nucleophilic attack on polymer backbone C–N bonds in aprotic solvents, enabling controlled, catalytic depolymerization to monomers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of P,N- or P,O-ligands for Ir(I) featuring pendant phenols, anilines, or ammonium salts, tuning their Brønsted acidity and spatial flexibility. These complexes will be screened in the catalytic deconstruction of model polyamides (e.g., Nylon-6) and polyurethanes in toluene or chlorobenzene, using silyl reagents (e.g., PhSiH3) as terminal reductants, analogous to C–H silylation. Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored by GPC and NMR spectroscopy. Key experiments will include kinetic isotope effects with deuterated pendant groups and comparison to intermolecular acid additives to prove the intramolecular shuttle mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for depolymerization catalysis by demonstrating the first molecular catalysts that integrate essential proton management directly into the ligand architecture for use in aprotic media. It will extend the principles of C–H functionalization catalysis, a Hartwig group strength, to the challenging realm of polymer deconstruction, providing a general strategy to bypass solvent limitations and access new recycling pathways.
proposals.novelty: While bifunctional catalysts for small-molecule activation are known, their application to polymer deconstruction is unexplored. The novelty lies in repurposing the mechanistic concept of metal-ligand cooperation—common in oxidation and N2 activation—specifically to stabilize the charged intermediates in backbone cleavage within an intentionally aprotic environment.

proposals.title: Aprotic Depolymerization of Polyesters via Catalytic Amidation Inspired by Solid-Phase Peptide Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Chemical recycling of condensation polymers like polyesters and polycarbonates is crucial for a circular plastics economy. Current catalytic depolymerization methods, such as alcoholysis or hydrolysis, rely on protic solvents and nucleophiles, which can degrade sensitive chemical functionalities in functionalized or post-consumer plastics. Developing catalysts that operate in aprotic media would provide superior chemoselectivity and enable the recovery of valuable, intact monomers from complex polymer streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that potent coupling agents and organic base catalysts from solid-phase peptide synthesis (SPPS), which drive amide bond formation in aprotic solvents, can be adapted to catalyze the reverse reaction: the nucleophilic cleavage of polymer backbones via amidation or transamidation in aprotic media.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of organocatalysts (e.g., phosphazene and guanidine superbases) and tailored metal-ligand complexes (focusing on Ir, Ni, and Cu with electron-donating, nitrogen-based ligands) for the depolymerization of model polyesters like PLA and PET using alkylamine nucleophiles in DMF. Reaction optimization will focus on catalyst loading, temperature, and nucleophile choice. Successful systems will be tested on functionalized and mixed plastic waste to assess selectivity and functional group tolerance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for solvent-controlled polymer deconstruction, moving beyond protic media. It will deliver novel catalyst designs that selectively cleave backbone bonds while preserving sensitive side-chain functionalities, expanding the toolbox for chemical recycling. The findings will bridge concepts from peptide chemistry to polymer sustainability.
proposals.novelty: The direct translation of SPPS coupling chemistry to polymer cleavage is unexplored. This approach is non-obvious as it inverts the conventional role of these catalysts from bond-makers to bond-breakers and explicitly rejects the necessity of protic solvents for depolymerization catalysis.

proposals.title: Photocaged Iridium Catalysts Integrated into Polymer Backbones for Light-Triggered, Catalytic Depolymerization
proposals.motivation: The development of chemically recyclable polymers is critical for a sustainable materials economy. Current approaches to triggered depolymerization often rely on inherently labile bonds, compromising material stability during use. A method to create polymers that are stable under ambient conditions but rapidly and selectively depolymerize on command would bridge the gap between performance and end-of-life recyclability.
proposals.hypothesis: That integrating photocaged, dormant iridium catalysts directly into a polymer backbone will yield materials with long-term stability, and that subsequent photolysis will uncage the catalyst, activating it to catalyze the selective cleavage of adjacent backbone bonds (e.g., via C–N or C–O oxidation/hydrogenolysis) and trigger rapid depolymerization to monomers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize monomers featuring an iridium complex ligated by a photolabile ortho-nitrobenzyl (o-NB) group on a key donor atom, deactivating the metal center. These monomers will be copolymerized with conventional monomers (e.g., via olefin metathesis or step-growth polymerization) to form stable materials. Depolymerization will be initiated by irradiating the solid polymer with 365 nm light, cleaving the o-NB group and generating an active Ir catalyst. This catalyst will then mediate a backbone-scission reaction, such as a dehydrogenative or oxidative process inspired by Hartwig's C–H borylation/functionalization expertise, leading to chain unzipping.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for spatiotemporally controlled polymer deconstruction, moving beyond backbone-instability strategies. It will demonstrate the first integration of photocaged transition-metal catalysts as structural elements for triggered depolymerization, providing a versatile design principle applicable to multiple polymer classes and catalytic degradation pathways.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious element is the decoupling of the trigger (light) from the depolymerization mechanism; light activates a dormant catalyst, which then performs the bond-breaking chemistry. This is distinct from photodegradable polymers where light directly cleaves the backbone, offering potential for greater material stability and catalytic amplification.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Synthesis and Triggered Degradation of Aryl-Amine-Functionalized Siloxanes via Aqueous Ammonia
proposals.motivation: The persistence of silicone polymers (siloxanes) in the environment is a major sustainability challenge. While catalyst-free depolymerization is highly desirable, it typically requires harsh conditions or specific triggers. Concurrently, the Hartwig group's development of KPhos-ligated catalysts for the amination of aryl iodides with aqueous ammonia provides a powerful, mild route to aromatic amines, a valuable polymer functionality. Merging these areas could enable intelligent materials designed for circularity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that siloxane polymers, strategically functionalized with aryl amine groups installed via aqueous ammonia amination, can be designed so that the same aqueous ammonia or its basic reaction conditions trigger the spontaneous, catalyst-free cleavage of the Si-O backbone, enabling full depolymerization on command.
proposals.method: We will first synthesize siloxane monomers and oligomers containing aryl iodide handles. Using the established KPhos/Pd catalyst system, we will perform C-N coupling with aqueous ammonia to install primary aryl amine functionalities. The molecular design of the siloxane linkage (e.g., incorporating sterically hindered or electron-withdrawing groups adjacent to the Si-O bond) will be iteratively optimized. The degradation will be studied by exposing the functionalized polymers to aqueous ammonia under mild conditions, monitoring Si-O bond cleavage via NMR, GPC, and mass spectrometry of the resulting silanol products.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for closed-loop polymer lifecycles, where the synthetic reagent also serves as the degradation trigger. It will deliver fundamental insights into the base-triggered scission of engineered Si-O bonds and provide a practical method to convert durable siloxane materials into readily degradable forms without added catalysts, advancing green polymer chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in using the product (aryl amine) and conditions of a powerful C-N coupling reaction as the intrinsic, built-in trigger for depolymerization, creating a chemically encoded self-immolative system from traditionally stable siloxanes.

proposals.title: Catalytic Post-Functionalization of Polyolefin Backbones via Amine-Directed C–H Borylation and Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in C–H amination of polymers, including Hartwig's work on polyethylene deconstruction via Beckmann rearrangement, create materials with pendant amine groups. However, the chemical potential of these installed amines for further, controlled polymer modification remains underexplored. Developing catalytic methods to use these amines as directing groups for selective C–H functionalization on the polymer side-chains would enable precise tuning of polymer properties like adhesion, compatibility, and reactivity from a common polymeric precursor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that primary amine groups installed along a polyolefin backbone can act as directing groups for subsequent, ligand-controlled iridium-catalyzed C–H borylation at adjacent methylene units. The resulting organoboron species will serve as versatile intermediates for oxidation to install diverse oxygen-containing functionalities.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model polyethylene substrates with spatially defined primary amines via Hartwig's established Beckmann rearrangement/hydrogenolysis sequence. Using these substrates, we will screen a library of electron-deficient, small-bite-angle bidentate ligands (e.g., modified bipyridines, phenanthrolines) in iridium-catalyzed C–H borylation, leveraging the amine's coordinating ability to achieve γ-selectivity. The optimal catalytic system (Ir precursor, ligand, conditions) will be applied to amine-functionalized polyethylene. The installed boronates will then be converted to alcohols via oxidation with sodium perborate or to phenols via one-pot ipso-hydroxylation of in-situ-generated aryl boronates from further modified sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, catalytic two-step platform for post-amination polymer editing, transforming C–H bonds to C–O bonds with sequence control dictated by the initial amine installation. It will demonstrate the utility of amine-directed C–H functionalization in polymer science, moving beyond small molecules. The resulting polyols and polyphenols are valuable materials for coatings, adhesives, and as precursors for further crosslinking or grafting.
proposals.novelty: While iridium-catalyzed C–H borylation directed by amines is known in small-molecule synthesis, its application to selectively functionalize the side-chains of a commodity polymer is unprecedented. The novelty lies in merging the concepts of polymer backbone editing with subsequent, directed C–H functionalization to achieve programmable, multi-step modification of polyolefin properties.

proposals.title: Visible-Light-Driven, Iridium-Catalyzed C-H Amination of Polyethylene via Hydrogen Atom Transfer
proposals.motivation: The chemical upcycling of polyethylene waste into higher-value nitrogen-containing materials, such as adhesives or compatibilizers, is a major sustainability challenge. Existing methods for polyolefin functionalization often require harsh conditions that degrade the polymer backbone. A mild, catalytic C-H amination protocol would enable the direct conversion of commodity plastics into functional polymers, aligning with the Hartwig group's recent focus on catalytic polymer deconstruction and modification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual catalytic system combining a photoredox iridium catalyst with a co-catalytic hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mediator can selectively abstract hydrogen from polyethylene's alkane chains, generating a carbon-centered radical that can be trapped by a nitrogen-centered radical acceptor under visible light irradiation at ambient temperature.
proposals.method: We will employ [Ir(dF(CF3)ppy)2(dtbbpy)]PF6 as the photoredox catalyst, leveraging its strong oxidizing power under blue LEDs. A thiol-based HAT co-catalyst (e.g., 2-mercaptothiazoline) will be screened to generate the key alkyl radical from low-density polyethylene (LDPE) films or particles. Nitrogen installation will be tested using electron-deficient sulfonyl azides (e.g., TsN3) or N-aminophthalimide as radical acceptors. Reaction optimization will focus on solvent (e.g., CH2Cl2), light intensity, catalyst loading, and polymer swelling agents to maximize the degree of functionalization while maintaining polymer integrity, as analyzed by IR, NMR, and GPC.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general method for the direct, catalytic C-H amination of unfunctionalized polyethylene under mild, photochemical conditions. It will expand the scope of catalytic polymer functionalization beyond borylation and silylation, providing a new route to amine-functionalized polyolefins with tunable surface and adhesive properties from waste feedstocks.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox C-H amination of small molecules is known, its application to inert, solid polyolefins is unexplored. The novelty lies in merging the group's expertise in iridium catalysis and polymer functionalization with photoredox-HAT manifolds to tackle the specific challenge of modifying long-chain alkanes in a heterogeneous or semi-heterogeneous system.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Iridium Catalysts for the Direct Amination of Polyethylene C–H Bonds
proposals.motivation: The chemical upcycling of polyethylene into value-added nitrogen-containing materials, such as adhesives or compatibilizers, represents a critical challenge in sustainable polymer chemistry. Pre-2026 work from the Hartwig group has demonstrated the catalytic borylation and backbone editing of polyethylene, but direct, catalytic C–H amination of this inert substrate remains unexplored. A general, selective method would transform waste plastic into functional polymers with amine handles for further modification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput screening (HTS) campaign of diverse ligand libraries under iridium catalysis will identify catalyst systems capable of mediating the direct, intermolecular amination of secondary C(sp3)–H bonds in model and authentic polyethylene chains with useful regioselectivity.
proposals.method: We will employ an automated liquid-handling robotic platform (e.g., Unchained Labs Junior or similar) to screen >1,000 ligand-metal combinations in parallel using 96-well plate arrays. The initial substrate will be a saturated, long-chain alkane (e.g., n-octadecane) as a soluble model for polyethylene. Reactions will utilize a nitrogen source (e.g., O-benzoyl hydroxylamine) under inert atmosphere. Product formation will be quantified via high-throughput GC-MS or colorimetric assays for amine detection. Hit catalysts will be evaluated on low-molecular-weight polyethylene samples using insights from the group's prior work on polymer functionalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first catalytic system for the direct amination of polyethylene, expanding the toolbox for plastic valorization beyond borylation and chain cleavage. It will establish a structure-activity relationship for ligands in this challenging transformation and provide a validated HTS pipeline for catalyst discovery in polymer C–H functionalization.
proposals.novelty: While HTS is common in pharmaceutical chemistry, its application to the catalytic functionalization of commodity polymers is rare. This approach is non-obvious because it adapts methods developed for small-molecule C–H amination to the unique challenges of a polymeric, solid-phase substrate, merging high-throughput discovery with polymer deconstruction.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed C(sp3)-H Borylation and Silylation of Amines Using Air-Stable Silicate and Borate Reagents
proposals.motivation: The catalytic functionalization of C-H bonds in amines using iridium catalysts is a powerful strategy for molecular editing, but it typically relies on hazardous, pyrophoric silanes (e.g., HSiMe(OR)2) and boranes (e.g., HBpin). These reagents pose significant safety and handling barriers, limiting the practical adoption of these transformations in both academic and industrial settings. Developing safer, air-stable alternatives would dramatically improve the operational utility of these catalytic C-H functionalization methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bench-stable alkali metal trialkylsilanolates (e.g., KOSiMe3) and alkali metal borohydrides (e.g., NaBH4) or boronate esters (e.g., NaBpin) can serve as effective silicon and boron sources in iridium-catalyzed C-H functionalization of amines, replacing pyrophoric hydride reagents while maintaining high regio- and stereoselectivity.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Hartwig group's expertise in iridium catalysis with bidentate nitrogen-based ligands to develop new catalytic systems. Initial studies will focus on the C-H borylation of primary alkyl amines using NaBpin as the boron source, optimizing the iridium precursor, ligand (e.g., phenanthroline derivatives), and base. Parallel investigations will target C-H silylation using KOSiMe3, probing the mechanism to understand if the reaction proceeds via a formal dehydrogenative coupling or an oxidative addition pathway. Substrate scope will be evaluated across diverse amine classes, with rigorous analysis of regio- and stereoselectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general protocols for C-H borylation and silylation using completely air-stable, non-pyrophoric reagents, significantly enhancing the safety profile of these reactions. It will expand the synthetic toolbox for amine functionalization and provide fundamental insights into the mechanism of C-H activation with alkali metal-based reagents, potentially uncovering new catalytic pathways.
proposals.novelty: While the use of alkali metal silanolates and borohydrides is known in stoichiometric deprotonation or reduction chemistry, their application as transmetalation partners in selective, catalytic C-H functionalization of amines with earth-abundant metal catalysts is unprecedented and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Modular, Transient Directing Groups for Regioselective Iridium-Catalyzed C(sp2)-H Borylation of Arenes and Heteroarenes
proposals.motivation: Directed C-H borylation is a powerful tool for regioselective functionalization, but current directing groups (DGs) often require multi-step installation and removal, limiting step-economy and substrate scope. A class of easily installed, catalytic, and traceless DGs would significantly streamline the synthesis of complex boronic esters, which are pivotal intermediates in pharmaceutical and materials chemistry. This addresses a key limitation in the widespread adoption of C-H borylation in synthetic planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weakly coordinating, heteroatom-based functional groups, capable of reversible binding to the iridium catalyst, can act as effective transient directing groups for C-H borylation and be removed in situ or under mild subsequent conditions.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of potential transient DGs based on pre-2026 knowledge of weak coordination, focusing on easily installed motifs like amides, sulfoxides, and N-oxides. These will be evaluated in iridium-catalyzed borylation reactions using [Ir(OMe)(cod)]2 with dtbpy or related ligands, screening for yield and regioselectivity. Successful DGs will be further optimized for in situ removal via hydrolysis or mild reduction post-borylation, establishing a one-pot installation/borylation/removal protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of practical, traceless directing groups for C-H borylation, directly expanding the synthetic utility of Hartwig borylation methodology. It will provide a more step-economical route to diverse aryl boronic esters and demonstrate a general design principle for transient coordination in C-H functionalization.
proposals.novelty: While transient directing groups are known for C-H activation with Pd and Rh, their application to Ir-catalyzed borylation is underdeveloped. The novelty lies in specifically designing DGs that leverage the unique coordination chemistry of Ir-borylation catalysts for reversible directing effects, moving beyond traditional, permanently installed DGs.

proposals.title: Late-Stage Diversification of Complex Bioactive Molecules via Iridium-Catalyzed C–H Borylation
proposals.motivation: The rapid generation of diverse analogs from a single, complex bioactive molecule is crucial for structure-activity relationship (SAR) studies and drug discovery. Traditional methods often require de novo synthesis or functional group interconversions, which are time-consuming and limit access to novel chemical space. A direct, catalytic method to selectively functionalize C–H bonds in late-stage intermediates would dramatically accelerate medicinal chemistry campaigns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iridium-catalyzed C–H borylation, leveraging tailored ligand and catalyst systems, can be applied to complex, drug-like molecules containing heteroatoms and sensitive functionalities to install boron handles with high regioselectivity. These handles can then be rapidly diversified via cross-coupling to generate focused libraries of analogs.
proposals.method: We will employ established iridium catalysts (e.g., [Ir(OMe)(cod)]₂) with sterically tuned bipyridine or phenanthroline ligands to achieve site-selective C(sp²)–H borylation on a panel of nitrogen-containing pharmaceuticals and natural products. Key to the approach is the systematic screening of ligand sterics/electronics and directing group strategies to override innate substrate bias. The generated aryl- and heteroarylboronic esters will be subjected to high-throughput diversification via parallel Suzuki-Miyaura and Chan-Lam couplings with commercial reagent sets, followed by purification via automated flash chromatography.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, catalytic platform for the late-stage diversification of bioactive scaffolds, moving beyond simple arene substrates. It will provide a practical toolkit for medicinal chemists to rapidly explore SAR. Furthermore, it will expand the scope of C–H borylation to densely functionalized, pharmaceutically relevant architectures, defining its limitations and opportunities.
proposals.novelty: While C–H borylation of simple arenes is well-known, its systematic application to complex, polyfunctional drug molecules with predictable regiocontrol remains a significant challenge. This proposal uniquely integrates Hartwig's expertise in catalytic borylation and ligand design with the specific demands of late-stage functionalization in drug discovery, a non-obvious extension of prior polymer and simple amine work.

proposals.title: Cobalt- and Nickel-Catalyzed C(sp3)-H Borylation and Silylation of Amines: Replacing Iridium in Hydroelementation Reactions
proposals.motivation: The Hartwig group's recent development of iridium-catalyzed, regio- and stereoselective C(sp3)-H silylation of amines represents a powerful method for amine functionalization. However, the reliance on precious iridium limits the scalability and sustainable application of these transformations. Replacing Ir with earth-abundant first-row transition metals would dramatically improve the economic and environmental profile of these catalytic C-H functionalization processes, aligning with broader goals in green chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that well-defined cobalt and nickel complexes, supported by tailored electron-donating and sterically demanding ligands, can catalyze the borylation and silylation of C(sp3)-H bonds in primary amines via a metal-hydride intermediate pathway analogous to precious-metal systems, achieving comparable regio- and stereoselectivity.
proposals.method: The approach will focus on synthesizing and screening libraries of low-valent Co and Ni complexes bearing bidentate phosphine (e.g., DPPE, Xantphos) and bis(carbene) ligands. Catalytic activity for the borylation and silylation of model primary amines (e.g., n-octylamine) with B2pin2 and PhSiH3 will be evaluated under varied temperatures and pressures. High-throughput experimentation (HTE) will identify lead catalysts, followed by detailed mechanistic studies using deuterium labeling, kinetic isotope effects, and stoichiometric reactions to probe the proposed metal-hydride insertion/elimination mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general earth-abundant metal catalysts for the direct hydroelementation of amine C(sp3)-H bonds, providing a sustainable alternative to Ir/Rh catalysis. It will expand the toolbox for amine editing and polymer deconstruction using base metals and offer fundamental insights into the reactivity of first-row metal-hydride species in challenging bond-forming steps.
proposals.novelty: While cobalt and nickel catalysts are known for C(sp2)-H borylation, their application to the selective functionalization of unactivated aliphatic C-H bonds in amines, a domain dominated by precious metals, remains largely unexplored and non-trivial due to competing side reactions at the nitrogen center.

proposals.title: Photoredox-Iridium Dual Catalysis for the Site-Selective C(sp3)-H Silylation of Aliphatic Amines
proposals.motivation: The direct, catalytic silylation of inert C-H bonds, as pioneered by Hartwig, provides a powerful route to versatile organosilicon building blocks. However, current methods often require elevated temperatures and can be limited by the need for directing groups or specific substrate classes. Merging this transformative C-H functionalization with the burgeoning field of photoredox catalysis could unlock new mechanistic pathways for C-Si bond formation under exceptionally mild, redox-neutral conditions, expanding the scope to sensitive and complex substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synergistic photoredox-iridium dual catalytic system will enable the room-temperature, site-selective silylation of primary and secondary C-H bonds in aliphatic amines via a mechanism involving single-electron oxidation of a substrate-derived intermediate, generating a key radical species for subsequent, ligand-controlled C-Si bond formation.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual catalytic system combining a well-defined iridium(III) complex (e.g., [Ir(cod)(OMe)]2 with a phenanthroline-based ligand) for C-H activation/silylation with a photoredox catalyst (e.g., fac-Ir(ppy)3 or an organic dye such as 4CzIPN). Reactions will be performed in a sealed vial under blue LED irradiation, using disilanes (e.g., PhMe2Si-SiMe2Ph) as the silyl source. Key variables to optimize include ligand structure (focusing on electron-donating groups to modulate Ir reactivity), photocatalyst redox potential, and solvent. Substrate scope will be evaluated on amines with varying chain lengths and proximal functional groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for C-H silylation that operates at ambient temperature, significantly broadening functional group tolerance. It will provide a detailed mechanistic framework for merging photoredox-mediated single-electron transfer with transition metal-catalyzed bond-forming steps, specifically for C-Si coupling. The resulting method will deliver synthetically valuable silylated amines under the mildest conditions reported.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox catalysis has been combined with C-H borylation, its application to C-H silylation is unexplored. The proposed system is non-obvious as it requires careful matching of the photoredox cycle with the distinct oxidative addition/reductive elimination steps of the iridium silylation catalyst to achieve productive synergy, rather than catalyst inhibition.

proposals.title: Enzyme-Inspired Ligand Pockets for Regioselective C(sp3)-H Borylation of Aliphatic Amines
proposals.motivation: The selective functionalization of unactivated C(sp3)-H bonds in complex molecules remains a paramount challenge in synthetic chemistry. While Hartwig's group has achieved remarkable regioselectivity in amine C-H silylation using iridium catalysts with directing groups, a general strategy for achieving predictable, remote selectivity without a directing group is lacking. Mimicking the controlled environment of an enzyme active site with synthetic catalysts would represent a transformative advance for late-stage diversification of pharmaceuticals and natural products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing sterically demanding, shape-persistent ligand scaffolds with defined, tunable pockets can enforce precise substrate orientation, thereby controlling the regioselectivity of C-H borylation in aliphatic amines without the need for a directing group.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of novel, C2-symmetric bidentate ligands featuring bulky, chiral dihydrobenzooxaphosphole or biphenyl backbones with substituents (e.g., 3,5-di-tert-butylphenyl, 9-anthracenyl) positioned to create deep, shape-selective pockets around the metal center. These ligands will be complexed to an iridium precursor (e.g., [Ir(COD)OMe]2) and evaluated in the catalytic borylation of model primary and secondary amines (e.g., N-Boc-n-hexylamine). Selectivity for terminal vs. internal C-H bonds will be mapped against pocket dimensions using a combination of X-ray crystallography, computational modeling (DFT for transition state geometries), and systematic steric parameter analysis (e.g., percent buried volume, %VBur).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for achieving enzyme-like regiocontrol in synthetic C-H functionalization catalysts, moving beyond traditional steric and electronic ligand effects. It will provide a modular ligand toolkit for predictable, remote C-H bond selection, directly impacting synthetic strategies in medicinal and polymer chemistry, as evidenced by Hartwig's recent work on polymer deconstruction.
proposals.novelty: While enzyme-inspired catalysis is known, applying a rigorous 'pocket engineering' approach to control selectivity in intermolecular, non-directed C-H borylation of flexible aliphatic amines with synthetic small-molecule catalysts is unprecedented. This moves beyond the established directing group paradigm in Hartwig's prior silylation work.

proposals.title: Iridium-Catalyzed, Aqueous-Compatible C–H Borylation for the On-DNA Diversification of DNA-Encoded Chemical Libraries
proposals.motivation: DNA-encoded library (DEL) technology is a powerful platform for ultra-high-throughput drug discovery, but its synthetic repertoire is limited by the need for aqueous-compatible reactions that can modify complex, unprotected scaffolds. C–H functionalization, particularly borylation, offers a direct route to introduce versatile boron handles for further diversification (e.g., Suzuki couplings), yet no catalytic method exists for performing such transformations on DNA-conjugated small molecules under DEL-compatible conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing an iridium catalyst system with a tailored, hydrophilic ligand, we can achieve efficient, regio- and stereoselective borylation of C(sp2)–H and C(sp3)–H bonds in DNA-linked substrates within aqueous-organic solvent mixtures, enabling a new class of on-DNA C–H functionalization for DEL synthesis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of bipyridine and phenanthroline-based ligands modified with polyethylene glycol (PEG) and charged groups for aqueous solubility. Using a model DNA-conjugated arene substrate, we will optimize reaction conditions (pH, co-solvent, temperature, iridium precursor, ligand, and boron source) for microscale (nanoliter volumes) borylation in 96-well plates. Success will be evaluated by LC-MS analysis of the DNA conjugate and conversion to the corresponding Suzuki-coupled product. The optimized protocol will then be applied to diversify a pilot DEL of aromatic and heteroaromatic cores.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general method for catalytic C–H borylation directly on DNA, significantly expanding the accessible chemical space in DELs. It will provide a robust, one-step route to install a versatile synthetic handle for downstream cross-coupling, accelerating the discovery of novel bioactive compounds.
proposals.novelty: While Hartwig's group has pioneered C–H borylation for polymers and small molecules, adapting this powerful transformation to the unique constraints of DNA-encoded chemistry—aqueous milieu, extreme dilution, and DNA compatibility—is an unprecedented and non-obvious challenge.

proposals.title: One-Pot Sequential C-H Borylation and Cross-Coupling for the Modular Functionalization of Polyolefins and Amines
proposals.motivation: The Hartwig group has pioneered catalytic C-H borylation and functionalization of polymers and amines, but these steps are typically performed in isolation. A one-pot, telescoped process integrating borylation with a subsequent cross-coupling would dramatically improve synthetic efficiency and atom economy for the valorization of hydrocarbon feedstocks. This approach is particularly compelling for upcycling polyolefins and modifying amine-containing pharmaceuticals, where minimizing purification steps is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed iridium catalyst system for C-H borylation, paired with a compatible palladium or nickel catalyst for cross-coupling, can operate sequentially in a single pot to directly convert C-H bonds into C-C or C-heteroatom bonds. The key is identifying orthogonal or sequentially activatable catalysts and ligands that tolerate the byproducts of the first transformation.
proposals.method: We will first develop conditions for the iridium-catalyzed C-H borylation of model substrates (e.g., linear alkanes, polyethylenes, primary amines) using B2pin2. We will then screen for a second catalyst (Pd or Ni) and ligand set that can be added directly to the borylation mixture to catalyze Suzuki-Miyaura or Chan-Lam couplings with aryl halides or amines, respectively. Critical parameters include solvent compatibility, order of addition, and the use of ligands (e.g., BrettPhos for Pd, bipyridine for Ni) that remain active in the presence of boron esters and amine byproducts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, one-pot platform for the dual functionalization of inert C-H bonds, moving beyond simple borylation to direct, complex derivatization. It will provide new, streamlined routes to functionalized polymers from plastic waste and to densely functionalized amine building blocks. The methodology will demonstrate the power of telescoped catalytic sequences in synthetic chemistry and polymer science.
proposals.novelty: While sequential catalytic reactions are known, a one-pot sequence combining these specific, challenging transformations (C-H borylation followed by cross-coupling on the same carbon) on substrates like polyolefins and amines is unprecedented. The novelty lies in the catalyst compatibility design to avoid intermediate isolation, directly addressing a major practical limitation in current C-H functionalization workflows.

proposals.title: Continuous-Flow, Iridium-Catalyzed Borylation of Amines in Microreactors for On-Demand Synthesis of Functionalized Intermediates
proposals.motivation: The catalytic borylation of C-H bonds, pioneered by the Hartwig group and others, provides versatile synthetic handles for downstream functionalization. However, these reactions often involve air- and moisture-sensitive reagents, pyrophoric catalysts, and generate hazardous byproducts like hydrogen gas, making scale-up and safe handling challenging. Microreactor technology offers superior heat and mass transfer, precise control over reaction parameters, and inherent safety for exothermic or gas-evolving reactions, which is ideal for managing these sensitive catalytic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing iridium-catalyzed C-H borylation reactions of amines in silicon-glass microreactors will enable the safe, efficient, and on-demand synthesis of borylated intermediates with improved selectivity and reproducibility compared to batch methods.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate silicon-glass microreactors with integrated mixing zones and temperature control. The catalytic system will be based on pre-2026 Hartwig group expertise, employing [Ir(OMe)(COD)]₂ with tailored bipyridine or phenanthroline ligands. We will develop a continuous process by separately pumping solutions of the amine substrate, the iridium catalyst/ligand, and bis(pinacolato)diboron (B₂pin₂) in an inert solvent (e.g., cyclooctane) into the microreactor. Parameters such as residence time, temperature, catalyst loading, and back-pressure (to manage H₂ evolution) will be systematically optimized using inline IR spectroscopy for real-time monitoring of conversion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, safe platform for the on-demand synthesis of valuable borylated amine building blocks, directly applicable to medicinal chemistry and materials science. It will demonstrate the translation of sensitive, homogeneous C-H functionalization catalysis into practical continuous-flow systems, contributing to the fields of green chemistry and process intensification.
proposals.novelty: While continuous-flow borylation of arenes has been reported, the application to the C(sp3)-H borylation of amines—a reaction with significant gas evolution and sensitivity—in chip-based microreactors is unexplored. The integration of ligand design, catalyst engineering, and microreactor fabrication for this specific transformation is a novel, interdisciplinary approach.

ideas.title: Replace palladium with a base metal catalyst using ligand field engineering from bioinorganic chemistry
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that palladium is uniquely necessary for aqueous amination by drawing an analogy from bioinorganic chemistry, where enzymes expertly use base metals like nickel and copper for sophisticated catalysis. The analogous solution is nature's use of precise ligand field engineering within a protein scaffold to control a base metal's reactivity. The adapted research idea is to design novel, sophisticated ligand architectures—inspired by metalloenzyme active sites—for nickel or copper catalysts. These tailored ligands would create a highly controlled electronic and steric environment, stabilizing the metal across necessary oxidation states and facilitating the key mechanistic steps of C-N bond formation under aqueous conditions, thereby enabling the replacement of palladium with a base metal catalyst.

ideas.title: Extend reaction to larger, more complex alkanes
ideas.description: Apply the catalytic system to functionalize C-H bonds in alkanes larger than C4 to test generality.

ideas.description: Use the principles to design catalysts for selective cyclization via C-H amidation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Mechanistic Insights into the Origins of Selectivity in a Cu-Catalyzed C-H Amidation Reaction.

ideas.title: Enzymatic Retro-Hydroxylation Strategy for C-N Bond Cleavage
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biocatalysis and metabolic engineering, where enzymes like cytochrome P450s are sometimes run in reverse through careful control of the reaction environment to perform retro-hydroxylation. The analogous solution involves engineering redox potentials and co-factor systems to favor the reverse of a standard functionalization. Adapted to the original catalyst system for aryl amination, the research would focus on identifying and applying the specific thermodynamic and kinetic levers—such as electrochemical potential, choice of halogenating agent, and use of sacrificial reagents—required to reverse the catalyst's pathway. The goal is to achieve selective C-N bond cleavage in primary arylamines, producing valuable aryl halides and completing a scalable, dual-purpose catalytic cycle for both bond formation and cleavage.

ideas.title: Scale reaction for continuous flow synthesis
ideas.description: Develop a continuous flow process for the iridium-catalyzed silylation to improve safety and scalability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Iridium-Catalyzed Silylation of Five-Membered Heteroarenes: High Sterically Derived Selectivity from a Pyridyl-Imidazoline Ligand.

ideas.title: Catalyst-Free, Triggered Depolymerization of Aryl-Functionalized Siloxanes via Aqueous Ammonia
ideas.description: This research direction combines the catalyst-free depolymerization of designed siloxanes with the expanded synthetic scope of aryl iodide amination. The core idea is to synthesize siloxane polymers where the siloxane backbone is functionalized with strategically chosen aryl amine groups, installed via the KPhos/aryl iodide amination protocol using aqueous ammonia. The molecular design of the siloxane linkages themselves will be tailored so that the presence of the aqueous ammonia product—or the basic conditions of its formation—serves as the specific environmental trigger that induces spontaneous cleavage of the Si-O bonds. This creates a closed-loop system where the same reagent (aqueous ammonia) both constructs the advanced aromatic functionality and, later, triggers the complete, catalyst-free degradation of the material on command.

ideas.title: Cooperative Bimetatalytic System for Mild and Selective Activation of Aryl C-F Bonds in Cross-Coupling
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the inherent inertness of aryl C-F bonds by employing a cooperative bimetallic catalyst system. A Lewis acidic metal pre-activates the fluoride, weakening the C-F bond and lowering the kinetic barrier for oxidative addition by a paired nickel catalyst. The system is engineered with carefully chosen ligands and additives to manage fluoride ion release and prevent catalyst poisoning. This enables selective cross-coupling of abundant aryl fluorides with a range of nucleophiles under synthetically mild, functional-group-tolerant conditions previously only possible with aryl chlorides or bromides.

ideas.title: Ligand-Free Palladium Amination via Surface-Immobilized Nanoreactors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a soluble phosphine ligand is necessary for selective palladium-catalyzed amination. Drawing an analogy from heterogeneous catalysis for environmental remediation, where metal ions are immobilized in functionalized porous matrices to perform selective reactions without ligands, we propose to engineer a ligand-free palladium system. The solution is to create 'nanoreactors' by atomically dispersing palladium within the nanopores of a hydrophilic, functionalized solid support (e.g., silica with tailored surface groups). In an aqueous medium, the C-N coupling substrates would diffuse into these pores. The confinement and specific surface interactions (e.g., with wall-bound amines or charged groups) would control the palladium's coordination geometry and reaction pathway, enabling selective amination without traditional phosphine ligands, mimicking the selectivity achieved through physical encapsulation in environmental catalysts.

ideas.title: Extend amination to other nucleophiles
ideas.description: Apply the solubility-control strategy to replace C–C bonds with C–O, C–S, or C–P bonds using different nucleophiles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Asymmetric Amination of Unstrained C(sp3)–C(sp3) Bonds

ideas.title: Directing Group-Enabled Chemoselective Arylation for Asymmetric Diarylamine Synthesis
ideas.description: This approach introduces a removable bidentate directing group onto the aryl electrophile substrate to enforce chemoselectivity. The directing group coordinates a metal catalyst (e.g., Cu, Ni, or Pd), creating a tailored pocket that facilitates oxidative addition and selectively positions the aniline coupling partner for reductive elimination, while excluding ammonia. After the selective cross-coupling, the directing group is cleaved under mild conditions to reveal the desired asymmetric diarylamine. This substrate-controlled method fundamentally bypasses the inherent reactivity bias, providing high selectivity and yield of the target molecule with minimal symmetric byproducts.

ideas.title: Ligand-Gated Catalytic System for Selective Aniline Coupling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the problem of substrate specificity in enzymatic catalysis to the synthetic challenge of selective diarylamine formation. In biochemistry, enzymes use precisely tailored active sites to discriminate between similar substrates. Here, we propose designing a transition metal catalyst system where a carefully engineered ancillary ligand creates a steric and electronic environment analogous to an enzyme's active site. This 'ligand-gated' catalyst would selectively favor the coupling reaction with aniline over ammonia by providing a binding pocket that stabilizes the transition state involving the bulkier aniline through shape complementarity, π-stacking, or directed hydrogen-bonding interactions, while disfavoring the smaller ammonia. This approach shifts selectivity from inherent reactivity differences to a designed, catalyst-controlled process.

ideas.title: Employ alternative inorganic bases as selective catalysts inspired by metalloenzyme cofactors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that hydroxide is the optimal or only viable inorganic base in aqueous systems. Drawing an analogy from bioinorganic chemistry, where metalloenzymes achieve remarkable selectivity by tuning the metal ion's first coordination sphere (e.g., with specific amino acid ligands), we propose to explore alternative inorganic bases that are not simple anions but structured complexes. The analogous solution involves moving from a simple metal-aquo complex to a designed coordination environment that controls reactivity. Adapted to this context, the research would involve synthesizing or selecting inorganic bases such as specific metal-ligand complexes (e.g., mimicking zinc hydrolase active sites) or polyoxometalates with well-defined structures and basic sites. These compounds would be tested in the target aqueous reaction system. Their tuned properties—such as modulated pKa, controlled nucleophilicity, specific shape, and ability to coordinate substrates—are hypothesized to offer superior control over reaction selectivity compared to hydroxide or simple salt bases, potentially enabling new synthetic pathways or improving yields of desired products in complex mixtures.

ideas.title: Invert selectivity from BDE to steric control
ideas.description: Design a catalyst to override bond dissociation energy trends and achieve amidation at sterically hindered sites.
ideas.sourceTitle: Copper‐Catalyzed Dehydrogenative Amidation of Light Alkanes

ideas.title: Bifunctional Aprotic-Solvent-Compatible Catalysts with Integrated Proton/Lewis Acid Shuttles for Controlled Depolymerization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach directly addresses the fatal flaw of insufficient intermediate stabilization in purely aprotic media. It focuses on developing molecular catalysts or designed active sites that contain a tunable auxiliary group (e.g., a pendant phenol, ammonium, or borane) capable of acting as an intramolecular proton or Lewis acid donor during the depolymerization transition state. This system retains the benefits of an aprotic solvent environment—such as wider thermal stability and compatibility with moisture-sensitive catalysts—while ensuring critical stabilization of reactive species. The result is a controlled, efficient depolymerization process that avoids catalyst poisoning and unwanted side reactions, broadening the scope of polymers that can be selectively recycled in non-protic systems.

ideas.title: Photoredox-Catalyzed Oxidative Deamination and Halogenation of Primary Arylamines
ideas.description: This redesigned approach addresses the thermodynamic hurdle of C-N bond cleavage by using photoredox catalysis to generate potent oxidants in situ, providing the necessary driving force. The system employs a dual catalytic cycle where an excited photocatalyst oxidizes the arylamine, facilitating N-centered radical formation and weakening the adjacent C-N bond. Concurrently, a halogen atom transfer (XAT) catalyst, activated by the photoredox cycle, delivers a halogen atom to the resultant aryl radical, yielding the aryl halide product. This design explicitly couples endergonic bond cleavage to exergonic oxidation and halogenation steps, enabling a net favorable transformation.

ideas.title: Microscale C-H borylation for DNA-encoded libraries

ideas.title: Micelle-Enabled Ligand Screening for Aqueous Cross-Coupling of Highly Hydrophobic Substrates
ideas.description: This research direction combines micellar catalysis with systematic ligand modification to tackle the challenge of cross-coupling extremely hydrophobic aryl halides in water. By employing tailored surfactant micelles to solubilize the substrates, a library of structurally varied KPhos-type ligands will be screened within this unique aqueous reaction environment. This approach will reveal how ligand sterics, electronics, and hydrophobicity influence catalytic activity and selectivity when the metal center operates at the micellar interface. The synergy enables the discovery of next-generation ligands specifically designed for high-performance catalysis in water, moving beyond simple solubility enhancement to true mechanistic optimization for sustainable chemistry.

ideas.title: Adapting Solid-Phase Peptide Synthesis Catalysts for Aprotic Depolymerization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that protic solvents are necessary for depolymerization catalysis by drawing an analogy from solid-phase peptide synthesis (SPPS). In SPPS, efficient bond formation (coupling) must occur in strictly aprotic solvents like DMF to prevent side reactions and resin degradation, solved by developing potent coupling agents (e.g., HATU) and organic base catalysts. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to design or discover depolymerization catalysts inspired by SPPS coupling chemistry. These catalysts would be active in aprotic media, potentially using organic super-bases or tailored organometallic complexes to polarize and cleave polymer backbone bonds (e.g., in polyesters or polycarbonates) without the need for protic solvents, enabling better control and compatibility with sensitive chemical functionalities.

ideas.title: Base Metal-Catalyzed C-F Amination in Aqueous Media
ideas.description: This research direction aims to develop nickel or copper catalytic systems for the direct amination of aryl fluorides in water or aqueous mixtures. The project will investigate how the earth-abundant metal's propensity for oxidative addition into C-F bonds can be enhanced and controlled in a protic aqueous environment, potentially through ligand design or synergistic metal effects. Success would establish a new, cost-effective, and green method to valorize chemically inert and abundant aryl fluoride feedstocks into valuable aniline derivatives. This approach combines the economic and sustainability benefits of base metals with the challenging activation of a strong bond, all within a water-based system.

ideas.title: Apply photoredox catalysis for C-H amination

ideas.title: Study inverse problem: C-N bond cleavage

ideas.title: Mediator-Controlled Electrochemical Palladium Catalysis for Selective Oxidative Transformations
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a tailored redox mediator, such as a stable organic nitroxyl radical or a metal complex, to regulate the potential at which the active Pd(II) catalyst is regenerated. The mediator is oxidized at the anode and then selectively re-oxidizes Pd(0) to Pd(II) in solution under mild conditions, effectively decoupling the catalyst's oxidation state from the harsh anode interface. This mediator-controlled cycle suppresses side reactions caused by high-potential anodic oxidation, significantly improving functional group tolerance and selectivity in oxidative Pd-catalyzed couplings. The system enables the use of lower catalyst loadings while maintaining high efficiency across a broader scope of complex substrates.

ideas.title: Polymers with Photocaged Catalytic Units for Triggered Depolymerization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that depolymerization triggers must directly break backbone bonds. Drawing an analogy from enzymology and prodrug design, where catalytic activity is controlled by photolabile protecting groups, we propose polymers designed for stability first, then triggered depolymerization. The polymer is synthesized to be highly stable in use, with its backbone integrity maintained. However, it contains dormant catalytic units integrated into its structure, which are deactivated by photocleavable 'cages'. A specific, external light trigger removes these cages, activating the catalysts within the polymer matrix. This activated catalyst then initiates a rapid and selective depolymerization process, converting the stable solid material back into its constituent monomers on demand, offering precise spatial and temporal control over polymer lifespan.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Nitrogen-Rich Support for Stabilizing Ligand-Free Palladium Nanoclusters in Aqueous Amination
ideas.description: This project investigates a heterogeneous strategy to overcome the fatal instability of ligand-free Pd in aqueous amination. It employs mesoporous graphitic carbon nitride (mpg-C3N4) as a support to stabilize sub-nanometer Pd(0) clusters, utilizing the material's nitrogen sites to prevent aggregation into inactive palladium black. The research will systematically correlate Pd cluster size and electronic interaction with the support to catalytic performance for challenging C–N couplings of aryl chlorides and bulky amines in water. The goal is to achieve a robust, phosphine-free catalytic system that maintains activity over multiple cycles by design, addressing the core deactivation mode of the original idea.

ideas.title: Design Trigger-Depolymerizable Polymers via Aqueous-Phase Catalytic Amination of Latent Nucleophiles
ideas.description: This research direction merges the concepts of triggered depolymerization and advanced aqueous-phase amination catalysis. The core idea is to synthesize polymers that are highly stable under use conditions but contain strategically placed, protected 'pro-nucleophile' functional groups installed via KPhos-catalyzed cross-coupling with challenging nucleophiles (e.g., hydroxylamines, hydrazines, or weak amines) in water. These dormant nucleophiles are chemically inert during the material's service life. At end-of-life, a specific external stimulus (such as UV light, a change in potential, or the addition of a small-molecule activator) cleaves the protecting group or otherwise activates the nucleophile. The now-reactive site intramolecularly attacks a nearby labile linkage in the polymer backbone (e.g., an activated ester or carbonate), initiating a cascade depolymerization event to monomers or small oligomers.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Photoredox Parallel Synthesis of Amine Libraries in Aqueous Microdroplets
ideas.description: This research direction combines miniaturized high-throughput synthesis in microtiter plates with aqueous photoredox catalysis to create a novel platform for parallel amine library generation. Light is used to activate a palladium catalyst or organic substrate within individual aqueous microdroplets, generating reactive radical intermediates for C-N bond formation. This setup allows for the simultaneous screening of hundreds of unique reaction conditions, including varied light wavelengths, catalysts, and substrates, in a single plate under mild, biocompatible aqueous conditions. The synergy lies in using light as a spatially and temporally precise external trigger to initiate diverse catalytic cycles in parallel microwells, dramatically accelerating the discovery of new photoredox amination reactions while minimizing reagent use.

ideas.title: Closed-loop active discovery of aqueous amination ligands via integrated computational design and microfluidic performance screening
ideas.description: This approach combines targeted in silico ligand design with high-throughput experimental catalysis in a machine learning-optimized loop to overcome the inefficiency of blind screening. A computational filter first generates a purpose-built virtual library enriched for aqueous-stable, electron-donating architectures relevant to amination. These designed candidates are then synthesized and tested for full catalytic performance—including turnover number and selectivity—using nanoliter droplet reactors that enable rapid, parallel reaction screening under realistic conditions. The resulting dataset trains a predictive model to guide the next cycle of library design, progressively focusing the search on ligands that are both synthetically accessible and functionally superior for the specific aqueous amination transformation.

ideas.title: Enzyme-Catalyzed Selective Diarylamine Synthesis for Biodegradable Polymer Design
ideas.description: This research fuses selective chemical synthesis with polymer biodegradation testing by employing a diverse panel of hydrolases and cutinases not just as degradative agents, but as catalysts for the selective coupling of anilines over ammonia to synthesize asymmetric diarylamines. The enzymatic conditions that successfully catalyze this selective bond formation will inherently reveal which enzyme active sites can recognize and interact with the specific amine products. This knowledge is then directly applied to design novel siloxane polymers incorporating these enzymatically-synthesized diarylamine linkers, where the same enzyme panel is used to test degradation, creating a closed-loop design principle for functional, biodegradable materials.

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Guided Design of Catalytic Ligands for Architecture-Specific Polymer Depolymerization
ideas.description: This research fuses computational ligand design with polymer depolymerization science to develop a targeted, data-driven approach for chemical recycling. Machine learning models will be trained on datasets linking polymer architectural features (e.g., branching, tacticity, functional groups) and ligand-metal complex structures to depolymerization efficiency and monomer purity outcomes. The core objective is to generate novel, aqueous-stable ligand designs optimized for the selective cleavage of specific bonds in target polymer architectures, such as polyolefins or polyesters. This enables the creation of tailored catalytic systems that dramatically improve yield and selectivity for complex, real-world plastic waste mixtures, moving beyond trial-and-error condition optimization.

ideas.title: Photolabile Protecting Groups for Siloxanes: Achieving Catalyst-Free Depolymerization via Light
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that depolymerization of siloxanes requires an added acid catalyst by drawing an analogy from photochemistry in organic synthesis. The analogous solution uses photolabile protecting groups that cleave spontaneously upon light irradiation without a catalyst. The adapted research idea is to design siloxane linkages with built-in photolabile chromophores. When exposed to specific environmental light conditions (e.g., UV light of a particular wavelength), these modified linkages will absorb photons and undergo an intramolecular photochemical rearrangement that directly cleaves the Si-O bond, enabling spontaneous, catalyst-free depolymerization of the polymer network.

ideas.title: Electrochemically Regenerated Palladium Catalysis for Structure-Adaptive Polymer Depolymerization
ideas.description: This research direction integrates electrochemical palladium catalysis with systematic studies of polymer structure-depolymerization relationships. The core concept is to use anodic oxidation to precisely regenerate and maintain the active Pd(II) species in situ, but to tailor the electrochemical conditions (e.g., potential, waveform) based on the specific polymer architecture being processed (e.g., linear vs. branched polyolefins, polystyrene, or condensation polymers). This allows for the optimization of depolymerization efficiency and monomer selectivity for each polymer type using a single, low-loading catalyst system, moving beyond one-size-fits-all conditions. The synergy enables a smart, adaptive electrochemical process that minimizes catalyst waste while maximizing monomer recovery yields across a mixed plastic feed.

ideas.title: Biphasic Microemulsion Photoredox Platform for Aqueous Palladium Catalysis
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses a thermodynamically stable, nanoscale biphasic microemulsion to spatially separate the photoredox and palladium catalytic cycles. The photoredox catalyst operates within hydrophobic nanodroplets, shielded from water, to efficiently generate reactive radicals or radical ions via light absorption. These lipophilic intermediates then rapidly partition into the continuous aqueous phase, where they are intercepted by a water-compatible palladium catalyst to drive the desired bond-forming reaction. This compartmentalization maximizes photoredox efficiency while maintaining the benefits of an aqueous reaction medium, significantly broadening the scope of compatible substrates and intermediates.

ideas.title: Correlate KPhos Ligand Architecture with Catalyst Decomposition Pathways in Aqueous Ammonia
ideas.description: This research will systematically modify KPhos ligand structures and directly monitor how each structural variant influences the dominant catalyst decomposition pathways (e.g., hydrolysis, oxidation, leaching) under aqueous ammonia conditions. By employing in-situ and operando spectroscopic techniques, the study will establish a structure-deactivation relationship (SDR) map, complementing the traditional structure-activity relationship (SAR). The ultimate goal is to identify ligand architectural features that actively inhibit deactivation mechanisms, leading to catalysts with inherently longer operational lifetimes and more predictable performance in demanding aqueous media.

ideas.title: Dually-Triggered Environmental Lock for On-Demand, Catalyst-Free Siloxane Depolymerization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach incorporates a stabilizing moiety directly into the siloxane monomer that acts as an 'environmental lock,' preventing premature cleavage. The lock is engineered to disengage only when two distinct, non-ubiquitous environmental triggers are applied concurrently, such as UV-A light exposure within a specific, mildly alkaline aqueous medium. This dual-trigger requirement ensures exceptional stability during material manufacture, use, and storage, while enabling precise, catalyst-free depolymerization on command at end-of-life under the designed combination of conditions.

ideas.title: Architecture-Dependent Triggered Depolymerization: Designing and Optimizing Polymers for On-Demand Circularity
ideas.description: This research program integrates polymer design with depolymerization optimization by creating a library of polymers that are chemically stable under use conditions but contain strategically placed, labile motifs responsive to a non-thermal external trigger (e.g., specific wavelength of light, electrochemical signal, or benign chemical catalyst). The core innovation is to systematically map how fundamental architectural parameters—such as backbone rigidity, side-chain sterics, crosslink density, and monomer sequence around the trigger site—govern both the material's performance stability and the kinetics, selectivity, and ultimate yield of the triggered depolymerization back to monomers. The outcome will be a set of design rules that allow for the *a priori* engineering of polymers with bespoke lifetimes and closed-loop recyclability, moving beyond trial-and-error to a fundamental science of circular polymer design.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Mechanistic Mapping of Ligand Water Compatibility for Amination
ideas.description: This research integrates automated, parallel synthesis and screening of diverse KPhos-like ligand libraries with real-time reaction analytics in aqueous ammonia. The high-throughput workflow systematically probes structural variables (e.g., sterics, electronics, solubilizing groups) under reaction conditions, generating a dense, multi-dimensional dataset. Machine learning models are then trained on this dataset to deconvolute and quantify the individual contributions of each structural feature to key performance metrics like catalytic activity, stability to hydrolysis, and ammonia tolerance. This approach moves beyond simple optimization to establish predictive design principles for phosphine ligands that are specifically robust and effective in aqueous, nucleophile-rich environments.

ideas.title: Immobilize KPhos on a structured monolith via organosilane grafting for flow synthesis
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from environmental engineering, specifically the design of monolithic catalysts used in automotive exhaust treatment. The analogous solution involves immobilizing active catalytic species on the high-surface-area washcoat of a structured ceramic or metallic honeycomb monolith, which is optimized for continuous flow, durability, and efficient transport. Adapted to this research context, the idea is to develop a heterogeneous KPhos catalyst by grafting the ligand onto a silica-coated monolith via a designed organosilane tether, followed by pallation. This creates a dedicated, plug-and-play flow reactor cartridge for the continuous synthesis of primary amines, combining the selectivity of KPhos with the mechanical and process advantages of a monolithic support system.

ideas.title: Predictive Design and High-Throughput Validation of Aqueous-Stable Catalyst Interfaces
ideas.description: This research integrates first-principles simulations and machine learning to predict atomic-scale catalyst decomposition at aqueous interfaces before experimental synthesis. An automated high-throughput platform then validates these predictions by screening candidate materials under operando electrochemical conditions, using coupled spectroscopy to monitor surface evolution in real time. The closed-loop system iteratively refines the computational models with experimental data, enabling the proactive design of catalysts with intrinsic resistance to aqueous deactivation. This approach shifts the paradigm from post-mortem analysis of failure to the predictive engineering of stability.

ideas.description: This research direction combines the material platform of siloxane-lactide ABA triblock copolymers with the catalytic capability of KPhos-ligated catalysts to perform aqueous-phase amination using challenging nucleophiles beyond ammonia. The goal is to directly functionalize the polylactide end blocks or modify the copolymer with amine-containing molecules (e.g., peptides, sugars, or drugs) in water, circumventing the need for organic solvents and complex protection/deprotection steps. This will yield a new class of amphiphilic copolymers with precisely located, covalently attached bioactive groups, tailored for applications such as drug-eluting coatings, cell-adhesive scaffolds, or antimicrobial surfaces where both controlled degradation and specific biological interactions are required.

ideas.title: Segmented Copolymers with Protected, Continuous Siloxane Blocks for Enhanced Stability
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates well-defined block copolymers featuring extended siloxane sequences within the backbone, but crucially embeds these blocks between robust organic polymer segments. The flanking blocks, made from monomers like substituted styrenes or acrylates, provide a hydrophobic and sterically shielding microenvironment that dramatically slows the kinetics of siloxane bond cleavage. This architecture allows the material to retain the key benefits of a continuous siloxane segment—such as high flexibility, low glass transition temperature, and gas permeability—while achieving the environmental stability necessary for practical applications in coatings, elastomers, or membranes.

ideas.title: Continuous Arylamine Synthesis via Catalytic Ammonolysis Inspired by Industrial Gas Processing
ideas.description: This research adapts the continuous, catalytic reactor solution from the industrial-scale Haber-Bosch process (ammonia synthesis) to the challenge of kilogram-scale continuous flow synthesis of primary arylamines. The analogous problem of achieving efficient, large-scale gas-phase reactions over a catalyst under pressure is solved by employing a robust heterogeneous catalyst in a pressurized tubular flow reactor with precise temperature zones and reactant recycling. The adapted idea is to develop a continuous process for manufacturing primary arylamines using aqueous ammonia by designing a kilogram-scale flow reactor system that utilizes a solid catalyst bed, operates at elevated pressure and temperature to enhance reaction kinetics and ammonia solubility, and incorporates heat exchange modules and an ammonia recovery/recycle loop to maximize efficiency and atom economy, mirroring the engineering principles of large-scale gas processing.
